Richmond Castle, Kalutara – Review

Richmond Castle is one of the most beautiful and historically fascinating places to visit in Sri Lanka. Located in Kalutara, this elegant mansion showcases a mix of British and Indian architectural styles, surrounded by lush greenery and calm gardens. The castle’s design, with its tall pillars, detailed carvings, and wide verandas, gives a glimpse into the luxury lifestyle of the early 1900s.

The environment is peaceful and well-maintained, making it a perfect spot for photography, family visits, or anyone interested in history and architecture. The staff is friendly and provides interesting background stories about the castle and its owner, Mudaliyar Don Arthur de Silva Wijesinghe Siriwardena.

Inside, visitors can explore the grand staircase, wooden floors, and antique furniture that reflect the colonial era’s charm. The surrounding garden and the scenic view of the Kalu River add extra beauty to the visit.

Overall, Richmond Castle is a must-visit heritage site in Kalutara — a place filled with history, beauty, and a calm atmosphere. 🌿

display_review:
Kande Viharaya is one of the most peaceful and spiritually rich places you can visit on Sri Lanka’s southwest coast. Located just a few minutes from Bentota and Aluthgama, this beautiful temple sits on a small hill surrounded by lush greenery — the name literally means “Temple on the Hill.”

The first thing you’ll notice is the towering Buddha statue, one of the tallest sitting Buddha statues in Asia. It’s an incredible sight, visible even from the main road, and looks especially breathtaking during sunrise or sunset. Inside the temple grounds, you’ll find beautifully detailed murals, ancient carvings, and quiet spots for reflection. The atmosphere is calm and welcoming, even if you’re not Buddhist — everyone is encouraged to explore respectfully.

The temple is well maintained, and the locals are kind and helpful if you need guidance or wish to make an offering. It’s a great place to learn a bit about Sri Lankan culture and Buddhist history while also enjoying some amazing views of the surrounding area.

If you’re visiting Bentota, Kande Viharaya is a must-see — peaceful, scenic, and spiritually uplifting. Go early in the morning or late afternoon to avoid the heat and experience the tranquility at its best.

Kande Viharaya Temple, located near Aluthgama in Sri Lanka’s Southern Province, is a deeply revered Buddhist temple with a rich cultural and spiritual legacy. The temple’s most iconic feature is its towering seated Buddha statue — one of the tallest in the world — which can be seen from miles away and evokes a powerful sense of peace and reverence.

The temple grounds are beautifully maintained, blending ancient traditions with modern devotion. Visitors can explore a series of vibrant murals and detailed frescoes that depict Jataka tales and scenes from the Buddha’s life, offering a visual education in Buddhist teachings. The atmosphere is serene, with the rhythmic chanting of monks and the scent of incense adding to the spiritual ambiance.

Kande Viharaya is not just a place for worship but also a cultural landmark, attracting pilgrims and tourists alike. The Bodhi tree, believed to be a sapling from the sacred Sri Maha Bodhi, holds special significance and adds to the sacred aura of the site.

Whether you're a spiritual seeker, a history enthusiast, or simply a curious traveler, Kande Viharaya offers a tranquil and meaningful experience. It’s a must-visit destination for anyone exploring the spiritual heart of Sri Lanka.

description: Buddhist temple dating back to the 14th century, with paintings, sculptures & carved inscriptions.
display_review:
Lankathilaka Maha Viharaya is one of the most beautiful Buddhist temples in Sri Lanka, located in Pilimathalawa, near Kandy. It was built in the 14th century during the reign of King Bhuvanekabahu IV of the Gampola Kingdom and stands majestically on top of a massive rock.

Recommendations:
Although the main entrance commonly used today is from the back, the old entrance is far more interesting. You will need to climb the ancient rock-cut steps, and you can also see the newly carved steps running alongside them. This old entrance is also accessible by vehicles, Take the route from the Lankathilaka M.V. (Lankathilaka Maha Vidyalaya) side. The road runs beautifully through the middle of the paddy fields.

Inside the temple, the main doorway is a massive wooden door, which is opened only once a year. For daily visitors, a smaller door built within the large one is used.

From the top, you get an almost 360 degree view at least a 300 degree panoramic view of the surroundings. If you look carefully from the stupa side, you can even spot the Ranawana Buddha statue in the distance.
review_1: An iconic, majestic white stupa (pagoda) located on the banks of the Kalu River, right at the northern entrance to Kalutara city. It is famous for being one of the world's few hollow Buddhist stupas, allowing visitors to walk inside its vast dome. The site is a highly venerated place of worship, located directly opposite the sacred Kalutara Bodhiya (Bo Tree). The Bo tree is believed to be a direct sapling of the Jaya Sri Maha Bodhi in Anuradhapura, which itself originated from the tree under which Lord Buddha attained enlightenment. The stupa and Bo tree create a powerful spiritual landmark at the entrance to Kalutara. Many travelers stop here to seek blessings for a safe journey. A must-see landmark in the region.
review_2: Kalutara bodhiya is one of my favorite places to visit Everytime I come to the Area. Temple is easily accessible and plenty of parking available separately. (You need to walk over the Mahanama Bridge though) Dagaba is one of a kind design in Sri Lankan Engineering.
review_3:
Visiting Kalutara Bodhiya was a peaceful and memorable experience. This sacred place has a calm and spiritual atmosphere that makes you feel relaxed the moment you enter the premises. The area is very well maintained, and the surrounding environment feels clean, serene, and welcoming to anyone seeking a quiet moment for prayer or reflection. The stupa itself is beautiful and impressive, and the fact that it is located so close to the river adds to the charm and uniqueness of the site.

One thing I really appreciated was how organized everything was. There are clear pathways, proper places to offer flowers, and volunteers who help guide visitors respectfully. Even when the crowd gets a little busy, it still manages to maintain a peaceful ambiance. The large Bo tree area is especially calming, with the soft sound of the wind and bells creating a soothing spiritual feeling.

Parking is generally convenient, and there are small shops nearby where you can buy flowers and other offerings at reasonable prices. I also noticed that the environment is kept clean, which makes the overall experience even better. Whether you are visiting for religious purposes or just to enjoy a bit of quiet time, Kalutara Bodhiya is definitely worth the stop. It’s a place that gives you both mental peace and a beautiful cultural experience.

display_review:
* Tranquil Location: Situated on the banks of the Kalu Ganga River, near the Dombagaskanda hill, the hermitage is surrounded by a lush 347-hectare wet zone rainforest. This natural canopy provides a peaceful and secluded environment ideal for meditation and spiritual practice.

* History: The hermitage was established in 1955, coinciding with the Buddha Jayanthi celebrations. Its founder is believed to be Olaboduwe Sri Revatha Dhamma Kitti Thera, who was also the principal of the Dharmadeepa Vipassana Pirivena in Kaluwamodara.

* Purpose: Bodhinagala Forest Hermitage serves as a retreat for Buddhist monks dedicated to meditation. However, it also welcomes visitors seeking tranquility and an opportunity to connect with nature and Buddhist teachings.

* Features: The hermitage includes kutis (small huts) for the monks, meditation pathways, and medical halls. A unique feature is the Gaudiya, a short tree trunk used as a bell to call monks to the main dining hall.
* Biodiversity: The surrounding forest reserve is home to diverse flora and fauna, including various bird species like the endemic Grey Hornbill, Rose-ringed Parakeets, and Hill Mynahs.

* Accessibility: It's reachable via the Panadura-Ratnapura highway (A8). From the highway, a short drive (about 1.5 km) along a minor road leads towards the Kalu Ganga and then another kilometer to the base of Dombagaskanda hill. While vehicles can go up, walking is recommended to enjoy the scenic forest paths.
In essence, Bodingala Forest Hermitage offers a tranquil escape into nature combined with a place of spiritual significance.

review_3:
brige of kalutara.

Sri Lanka, also known as the Pearl of the Indian Ocean is adorned with the beautiful mother nature and claims a proud history going back as far as 500 BC. Colonialism plays a significant role in the country’s olden times.

It was under the influence of Portuguese, Dutch and the British in their respective eras. Several historic sites in Sri Lanka originated during these periods and still remains as evidence of these influences. The Kalutara Bridge is such significant landmark inherited as a result of the Colonial period.

Kalutara bridge is the connecting line between the western and southern borders of the country. J. R. Mosse, Director of the PWD (Public works department) is the pioneer in designing and building the bridge and it was the largest bridge on the island built during the early 1900s. It was considered the first concrete bridge in Sri Lanka as well.

Locals say on very clear mornings one can see Sri Pada (Adam’s Peak) Mountain from the bridge. Which is, in turn, the starting point of Kalu River. That would make Kalu River the only river in Sri Lanka of which starting point and ending point can be seen at the same time.
The old bridge comprised of two separate bridges, each with 6 spans of lattice girders where a single girder was an enormous 100ft long.

It is separated by a ridge in the middle of the river (Kalu Ganga) and it was opened for road traffic in August 1877 along with a single line of rails in the middle. It was 450ft in length.

Currently, There are twin bridges for railway and one for the road traffic. And it is a perfect place to watch the sunset.

review_2:
This is a place of great historical and spiritual significance. According to the rock inscription found here, it dates back to the 30th regnal year of King Parakramabahu I, placing it in the 12th century A.D. The site is officially recognized by the Ministry of National Heritage and is under the care of the Department of Archaeology (Puravidya Department).

The inscription mentions that the land belongs to the Buddhist monastic community. A notable feature of the site is the stupa, which is said to enshrine the sacred relics (dathu) of Arahant Maha Kashyapa, a revered monk known for his deep spiritual discipline (Duthanga practices). He is especially honored in Buddhism for bearing a resemblance to the Buddha in appearance.

This site is not only a valuable piece of history but also a merit-giving sacred space for Buddhists. Whether you come for religious reasons or simply seek a quiet and peaceful environment, this place offers a calm, serene atmosphere that is perfect for reflection and worship.
review_3:
Galagoda Shailatharama Temple History

The Galagoda Shailatharama Viharaya is a sacred place of worship.
It is believed that this temple, located in the calm village of Karandeniya, was established during the reign of King Parakramabahu VI of the Kotte Kingdom (early 15th century).
During this period, a noble monk who came from India resided at this location and propagated the teachings of the Buddha.
It is said that the temple was later expanded and protected by many kings and local leaders.

The most significant feature of the Galagoda Shailatharama Viharaya is the massive reclining Buddha statue built within the temple premises.
This Buddha statue, made out of brick and clay, measures about 35 meters (115 feet) in length and is considered one of the largest reclining Buddha statues in South Asia.
In addition to the main statue, there are several smaller statues of standing and seated Buddhas, as well as beautiful paintings depicting scenes from the life of the Buddha and Jataka stories.

The temple has a rich collection of ancient murals and wood carvings that display the exceptional craftsmanship of the past.
The paintings mainly illustrate religious stories and teachings aimed at guiding devotees along the path of Dhamma.
It is believed that these works of art were created by artists belonging to the Kandyan period (17th–19th centuries).

Despite various threats over the centuries—including colonial invasions and natural disasters—the monks and devotees of the region have continuously worked to protect and preserve the temple.
They have maintained the temple’s structure, its valuable paintings, and the reclining Buddha statue with great devotion.

Today, the Galagoda Shailatharama Temple stands as a beacon of Buddhist faith and culture, attracting pilgrims and visitors from across Sri Lanka and abroad.
Devotees who visit the temple are reminded of the deep historical and religious significance of the site and are encouraged to follow the noble teachings of the Buddha towards the attainment of Nibbana.

May all who visit here be blessed with peace, happiness, and spiritual progress.

---------------------------------------------------

This sacred site, the Galagoda Shailatharama Viharaya, is renowned for housing one of the largest reclining Buddha statues built of brick and clay in Sri Lanka.
It is recorded that the temple and the Buddha statue were established during the reign of King Parakramabahu VI of the Kotte Kingdom.
According to local tradition, the temple was first founded by a monk who came from India and settled here, choosing this peaceful environment to spread the teachings of the Buddha.

place_name: The Royal Palace of Kandy (Maha Wasala)
description: Built circa 1634, this palace was home to the monarch of the Kingdom of Kandy until the early 1800s.
display_review:
The Old Royal Palace, located within the precincts of Sri Dalada Maligawa (Temple of the Tooth Relic) in Kandy, is a fascinating historical site that offers a glimpse into Sri Lanka’s royal past. The palace, which once served as the residence of the Kandyan kings, is an integral part of the temple complex and holds significant cultural and historical value.

The Old Royal Palace was originally built in the 16th century during the reign of King Vimaladharmasuriya I. It was constructed to accommodate the king and his court, reflecting the grandeur and elegance of the Kandyan era. The palace complex was strategically positioned to be close to the Temple of the Tooth Relic, symbolizing the close relationship between the monarchy and the Buddhist faith.

description: An ancient and sacred Buddhist temple in Kandy, reflecting Sri Lanka's deep religious heritage.
display_review:
Gadaladeniya Rajamaha Viharaya with an ancient monastery is found on the flat rock at Diggala in the district of Kandy. According to an inscription, this temple was built by King Buwanekabahu IV in the year 1344. The chief architect of this temple has been a South Indian called Ganesvarachari. Therefore this temple is essentially built following the Indian Vijayanagar architectural style.

The vihara complex in Gadaladeniya is built over a rock plateau spreading from north to south. According to the Gadaladeniya inscription by Dharmakirti, the Devala at Gadaladeniya had been constructed for the protection of the vihara.

The main shrine room has a seated (in Vajrasana) Buddha Statue under a Makara Thorana and four standing Buddha images. The makara thorana is decorated on both faces with gods such as Brahma, Suyama, Santhusuta, Natha and Maithree, and two attendants.

The chamber within the Sikhara (dome) has had a Buddha Image but has been destroyed by the Portuguese. Next to the shrine room is a Devale (shrine) dedicated to God Visnu. According to Mahavansa, The great chronicle of Sri Lanka, It was “Upulvan Deiyo” thus Visnu who was selected as the guardian to protect the land of Sri Lanka and Buddhism within it at the time of Buddha’s passing away. Therefore this deity has a special place among the Sri Lankans.

By the side of the Visnu Devale is a Stupa made of a rock. This is believed to have been constructed by King Parakramabahu V. This stupa has a roof built over it held on 4 pillars. 4 smaller satellite stupas are built on small shrine rooms around the main stupa. This structure is called the Vijayothpaya or Vijayantha Prasada named after the mythical palace of god Indra.

This temple was neglected for some time and then it was handed over to the Velivita Saranankara Thero by King Viraparakrama Narendrasinha (1707 – 1739) and this temple has been looked after ever since by his pupillary succession.

Though this temple is known as “Gadaladeniya” today, An inscription identifies this temple as “Dharma Kirthi Viharaya” which is the name of the founding monk. The work “Nikaya Sangrahaya” identifies this temple as “Saddharmathilake” and “Saddharmalankaraya” identifies this as “Gadaladeniya Viharaya” after the village in which it is located.

review_2:
The Gadaladeniya Raja Maha Viharaya dates back to the 14th century CE built by King Bhuvanaikabahu IV of the Gampola kingdom.
As with other temples in the area, Gadaladeniya was also designed by a Tamil architect and hence the strong influence of Dravidian architecture in its appearance.
In the shrine room which is entirely sculpted in granite is a eight foot high Buddha in meditation (Dhyana mudra).
Adjacent to the shrine room is a Devalaya dedicated to Lord Vishnu who is believed to be the protector of Buddhism in the country.

review_4:
When the Google map was followed we were directed through a poor quality road. It is best to ask from the villagers where the temple is than follow the Google map.
There is free parking at the entrance and ample parking area.

The temple is situated on a rock and it is a beautiful sight.
There are booklets to buy regarding the history of the temple.

Sadly, the place is very poorly maintained and the paintings inside the temple left to decay. The colour had peeled off and it is hardly conserved. A new roof had been built over the main temple area where the statue of the Lord Buddha is kept.
There are two main stone inscriptions which are also left to decay and hardly preserved.
Attention needs to be drawn to better preservation of these priceless efforts from our ancestors.

description: Buddhist temple dating back to the 14th century, with paintings, sculptures & carved inscriptions.
display_review:
Lankathilaka Maha Viharaya is one of the most beautiful Buddhist temples in Sri Lanka, located in Pilimathalawa, near Kandy. It was built in the 14th century during the reign of King Bhuvanekabahu IV of the Gampola Kingdom and stands majestically on top of a massive rock.

Recommendations:
Although the main entrance commonly used today is from the back, the old entrance is far more interesting. You will need to climb the ancient rock-cut steps, and you can also see the newly carved steps running alongside them. This old entrance is also accessible by vehicles, Take the route from the Lankathilaka M.V. (Lankathilaka Maha Vidyalaya) side. The road runs beautifully through the middle of the paddy fields.

Inside the temple, the main doorway is a massive wooden door, which is opened only once a year. For daily visitors, a smaller door built within the large one is used.

From the top, you get an almost 360 degree view at least a 300 degree panoramic view of the surroundings. If you look carefully from the stupa side, you can even spot the Ranawana Buddha statue in the distance.

description: Hindu temple built between the 14th & 15th centuries, featuring ornately carved wooden pillars.
display_review:
What is ambakke
"Ambakke" most likely refers to Embekke Devalaya (also spelled Ambekka Devalaya), a historical temple complex in Sri Lanka famous for its exquisite wood carvings.

It is a significant site for Sri Lankan art and architecture, located in the Udunuwara area of the Kandy District.

Here are the key details about Embekke Devalaya:

Famous for Wood Carvings: The temple is renowned for having some of the finest wood carvings in Sri Lanka and possibly the world, especially in the Drummers' Hall (Hevisi Mandapaya).

Historical Period: It was primarily built during the Gampola Era (14th century), attributed to King Vikramabahu III.

Deity: It is dedicated to the Kataragama deviyo (also identified with the Hindu God Skanda or Murugan), a prominent guardian deity in Sri Lanka.

Architecture: The Drummers' Hall is a marvel of ancient carpentry, featuring ornate wooden pillars and a unique structural element called the Madol Kurupuwa, a single wooden pin that holds 26 rafters of the roof together.

Carving Motifs: The carvings depict a huge variety of subjects, including:

Mythical creatures (like the entwined swans, double-headed eagles, and the elephant-bull hybrid).

Human figures (dancers, wrestlers, soldiers on horseback, and a mother nursing a child).

Decorative patterns (floral and geometric designs).

Would you like to know more about the specific wood carvings or perhaps its location and visiting information?

What is ambakke
"Ambakke" most likely refers to Embekke Devalaya (also spelled Ambekka Devalaya), a historical temple complex in Sri Lanka famous for its exquisite wood carvings.

It is a significant site for Sri Lankan art and architecture, located in the Udunuwara area of the Kandy District.

Here are the key details about Embekke Devalaya:

Famous for Wood Carvings: The temple is renowned for having some of the finest wood carvings in Sri Lanka and possibly the world, especially in the Drummers' Hall (Hevisi Mandapaya).

Historical Period: It was primarily built during the Gampola Era (14th century), attributed to King Vikramabahu III.

Deity: It is dedicated to the Kataragama deviyo (also identified with the Hindu God Skanda or Murugan), a prominent guardian deity in Sri Lanka.

Architecture: The Drummers' Hall is a marvel of ancient carpentry, featuring ornate wooden pillars and a unique structural element called the Madol Kurupuwa, a single wooden pin that holds 26 rafters of the roof together.

Carving Motifs: The carvings depict a huge variety of subjects, including:

Mythical creatures (like the entwined swans, double-headed eagles, and the elephant-bull hybrid).

Human figures (dancers, wrestlers, soldiers on horseback, and a mother nursing a child).

Decorative patterns (floral and geometric designs).

Would you like to know more about the specific wood carvings or perhaps its location and visiting information?

review_2:
British Garrison Cemetery

British Garrison Cemetery (also known as the Kandy Garrison Cemetery) is a British cemetery in Kandy, Sri Lanka, for British nationals who died in Ceylon. It was established in 1817 just after British captured the Kandy and closed in 1873 due to a ban on burials within the municipal limits, although special provision was given to allow the burial of relatives of those interred in the cemetery, with last person buried there being Annie Fritz in 1951. The cemetery contains 195 graves of men, women and children. The most common causes of death were tropical diseases such as malaria and cholera.

The cemetery was restored in 1998 using financial contributions from the Commonwealth War Graves Commission and is currently maintained by a group called 'The Friends of the British Garrison Cemetery in Kandy'. The cemetery is located within the land of the Sri Dalada Maligawa, the cemetery is maintained by the British while the Diyawadana Nilame and the Chief Prelates maintain the land.

Rating: ★★★☆☆ (3/5)

I recently visited the Asgiri Maha Viharaya in Kandy, and overall, it was an interesting and peaceful experience, though there were some aspects that could be improved.

The temple offers a beautiful and authentic insight into Sri Lanka’s Buddhist traditions. It’s one of the two main monastic orders in Kandy and has significant historical importance. The architecture is traditional and serene, and the quiet atmosphere gives you a moment to reflect and observe. The surroundings are quite peaceful compared to some of the more crowded tourist spots in the area. You can sense the spiritual energy and see how the monks live and practice their faith.

However, I would say the experience felt a bit limited for visitors. There wasn’t much information available in English, so unless you come with a guide or do prior research, it’s hard to understand the full historical or religious context. Also, while the place is definitely authentic, it isn’t as well-maintained or welcoming as other temples we visited in Sri Lanka.

It’s not a typical tourist attraction – and that’s both a strength and a weakness. If you’re deeply interested in Buddhism or local culture, it’s worth a visit. But for more casual visitors, it might not be very engaging without background knowledge.

In summary, Asgiri Maha Viharaya is a quiet and genuine place with cultural value, but it could be improved in terms of accessibility for international visitors. Still, I appreciated the chance to see a different side of Kandy and experience a more spiritual environment.

review_2:
Ranawana Purana Raja Maha Viharaya dates back to the 14th century CE built by King Bhuvanaika IV during the peak of the Gampola kingdom. This period was marked by a shift to South Indian styles of architecture and the Ranawana Viharaya is no exception.
At the entrance to the temple is an impressive archway, with intricately carved motifs of religious and cultural significance.
The temple itself blends with nature and is home to a giant statue of the walking Buddha, which at 80 feet is the tallest such statue in the world. The raised right hand (abhya mudra) is reassurance of his protective and compassionate nature.
On either side of the rather steep stairway, which leads up to this statue, there are 28 statues of the Buddha seated in meditation under different types of trees. Paying homage to the Buddha are statues of 80 of his most prominent disciples.
The route is clearly marked from entrance to exit and the garden is very well maintained.
Overall, the visit to the temple was an awe-inspiring spiritual experience.

The Kandy National Museum is a must-visit for anyone interested in the rich cultural and historical heritage of Sri Lanka. Located near the iconic Temple of the Tooth Relic, the museum provides a deep dive into the Kandyan era and the broader history of the island.

The museum is housed in a beautifully preserved building that was once part of the royal palace complex, adding to the authenticity of the experience. Inside, you’ll find an impressive collection of artifacts, including ancient weapons, ceremonial items, traditional Kandyan costumes, and intricately carved objects that reflect the craftsmanship of the time.

What I particularly enjoyed was the attention to detail in the displays. Everything is well-organized, with detailed descriptions that explain the significance of each exhibit. The museum offers a thorough look at the culture, history, and artistry of the Kandyan kingdom, as well as Sri Lanka’s colonial period.

For anyone exploring Kandy, I highly recommend a visit to the Kandy National Museum. It’s a fantastic way to understand the historical context of the area and appreciate the depth of Sri Lanka’s past. A perfect complement to visiting the Temple of the Tooth Relic!

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Kandy, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
St. Paul's Church, Milagiriya iss truly a place that offers both spiritual solace and historical richness. It is located in the heart of Bambalapitiya, Colombo, this Anglican Church has been a significant part of the local community for over 130 years. The serene atmosphere here makes it not only a place of worship but also a spot for peaceful reflection.

Originally established by the Portuguese as a Roman Catholic church, it was called "Nossa Senhora dos Milagres," which translates to "Our Lady of Miracles." The name Milagiriya is derived from the Portuguese word "Milagres," referring to miracles, and it’s said that a well near the original church was known for its supposed healing properties. People from across Sri Lanka would come here seeking spiritual and physical healing, which adds a layer of intrigue and reverence to the site.

The church today is part of the Diocese of Colombo and serves a diverse congregation from various walks of life, all united by their Christian faith. Whether you're a local or a visitor, there’s a real sense of belonging when you step inside. The church not only provides a space for worship but also plays an active role in guiding its members with Christian values.

review_4:
Nestled in the heart of Kandy, Sri Lanka’s picturesque "Hill Capital," St. Paul’s Church stands as a testament to the island's rich colonial history and religious diversity. Sharing a boundary with the revered Temple of the Tooth (Dalada Maligawa), this historic church adds a unique dimension to the spiritual landscape of Kandy.

The inception of St. Paul’s Church dates back to 1825, sparked by the visit of the second Bishop of Calcutta, the Rt. Rev. Reginald Heber. This visionary visit set the stage for the church's construction, which began in 1843. The cornerstone was laid on the 16th of March, 1843, by the Rt. Rev. Dr. Spencer, the Bishop of Madras (now Chennai). Although still under construction, the church opened its doors for worship in 1846, providing a sanctuary for the local community and British regiments, notably the 15th and 37th Regiments.

The church was formally consecrated on St. Paul’s Day, 25th January, 1853, by the first Bishop of Colombo, the Rt. Rev. James Chapman, and was aptly named “St. Paul’s.” The building itself, constructed entirely of terracotta brick, exudes a timeless elegance that has endured for generations.

One of the most striking features of St. Paul’s Church is its magnificent stained glass window, a gift from the widow of Laurence St. George Carey, a tea planter on Le Vallon Estate, Pupuressa, in 1874. This exquisite window, depicting the Crucifixion, the Ascension, the Angel in the Tomb, and the Nativity, was erected above the High Altar, enhancing the church's beauty and spiritual ambiance.

Another notable aspect of St. Paul’s Church is its historic pipe organ, which significantly enriched the quality of worship for over 125 years. Despite experiencing frequent breakdowns and a period of silence, the lower keyboard was diligently restored, allowing the organ to play until it was ultimately replaced in 2009.

St. Paul’s Church is not just a place of worship but a living museum of history, art, and architecture. Its intricate stained glass windows, robust terracotta brick structure, and the resonant tones of its once-majestic pipe organ collectively tell the story of a bygone era. Visitors to Kandy will find that a visit to St. Paul’s Church offers a profound connection to Sri Lanka's colonial past and a deeper appreciation for its cultural heritage. Whether you are a history enthusiast, an architecture aficionado, or a spiritual seeker, St. Paul’s Church is a must-visit destination that promises to leave a lasting impression.

Hindagala Raja Maha Viharaya is picturesquely situated on a rock close to the University Campus at Peradeniya along the Galaha Road. It has rock inscriptions dating back to the 6th century that speaks of the history of this temple. Amidst the ruined temple paintings of the 6th century are found paintings on the walls of the temple belonging to different periods of recent history.

The cave shrine and monastery known as Hindagala Raja Maha Viharaya is situated in the Kandukarapahala Korale of Udapalatha Division of Kandy District. The cave shrine is situated on a hill commanding a panoramic and idyllic of the hill ranges belted the Mahaveli river valley about 1.5 kms, away from Peradeniya University turns off on Peradeniya-Galaha Road. The shrine has to be reached through a steep ascent of 50m from the ground level, through a motorable narrow pathway and a flight of steps, in an utter state of despair, with stones of uneven surface. The hill on a clear day gives a view of the Adam’s Peak, hence, being selected in ancient times as residence of meditative monks in the the pre Christian eras.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Kandy, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
Adahanamaluwa gedige temple which is also known as Asgiriya temple/Asgiriya gedige, is a Buddhist monastery located in Asgiriya in the World Heritage city of Kandy in the Central province of Sri Lanka.

It is the headquarters of the Asgiriya chapter of the Siyam Nikaya, one of two Buddhist monasteries which holds the custodianship of the Sacred Tooth relic of the Sri Dalada Maligawa Temple.

The Asgiri Maha Viharaya traces it's origin back to the Wanawasi sect of the Dimbulagala Forest Monastery of the Polonnaruwa era.

This temple was founded in the era of King Parakramabahu IV (1305-1335 A.D.) and is believed to be considerably older than the Malwathu Maha Viharaya which is the other monastic centre of the Siyam Nikaya.

Asgiriya temple houses within it's premises the historically significant 'Adahana maluwa' or the Royal Cremation Ground of the Kandyan kingdom where the members of royal families were cremated and remains buried.

Historically, this gedige structure with it's unique ancient architectural features is the second oldest building in Kandy city.

Located in the hub of the Kandy city, it's a few minutes walking distance from the Temple of the Tooth relic.

Must visit for the history lover when in Kandy, Sri Lanka.

review_2:
​The Nelligala International Buddhist Centre, often simply referred to as Nelligala Temple, is a captivating spiritual site perched atop a hill in the Muruthalawa village, just a short drive from Kandy. While many temples in the area boast ancient heritage, Nelligala stands out for its blend of modern architecture, striking golden statues, and, most famously, its breathtaking 360-degree panoramic views.
​Architectural Splendor and Serenity
​What immediately strikes visitors is the temple’s clean, modern, and expansive design. Unlike the intricate, centuries-old stone carvings of traditional Sri Lankan temples, Nelligala features large, open grounds that complement the bright, golden statuary.
​The site is dominated by several key features:
​Golden Stupas and Statues: The temple houses a majestic Golden Stupa and several impressive Golden Buddha statues (often depicting the Vitarka Mudra, or "gesture of discussion"). These features gleam brilliantly against the blue sky, creating a visually dramatic and deeply serene contrast.
​The Bo Tree in a Golden Bowl: A unique symbolic touch is the sacred Bodhi Tree, which is often housed in a large, ornamental golden bowl, adding to the site's rich spiritual significance.
​Statue of God Sumana Saman: Look for the awe-inspiring statue of the deity Sumana Saman seated on a white elephant, which is a key pilgrimage sight and a beautiful piece of craftsmanship.
​The use of golden structures and open space provides a feeling of grandeur and tranquility, making it feel less like a historical museum and more like a vibrant, contemporary place of worship and reflection.
​The Unmatched View
​The single biggest draw of Nelligala is the spectacular location. Situated 750 meters above sea level, the hilltop location provides uninterrupted 360-degree vistas of Sri Lanka’s central highlands.
​On a clear day, visitors can spot famous peaks and landmarks in the distance, including the Hanathana Mountain Range, Alagalla, Batalegala, and even the distant peak of Sri Pada (Adam's Peak). The sweeping views of lush green valleys, paddy fields, and distant forests are a photographer's paradise.
​Pro Tip: Plan your visit for sunrise or sunset. The "golden hour" light reflecting off the gilded statues and stupas, combined with the vibrant colors of the sky, is an unforgettable, almost magical experience. Early mornings also tend to be less crowded and offer clear views before the haze sets in.
​Experience and Accessibility
​Nelligala is highly accessible compared to many other hilltop temples. Paved roads lead almost all the way to the summit, and the premises are well-maintained with good facilities.
​Peace and Tranquility: The primary feeling here is one of peaceful respite. It's an excellent location for meditation, contemplation, and escaping the hustle of Kandy city.
​Inclusivity: The site is designed to be welcoming to people of all faiths and offers facilities like ample parking and wheelchair access, ensuring that the spiritual experience is available to everyone.
​Dress Code: As with all Buddhist temples in Sri Lanka, visitors should dress respectfully, covering their shoulders and knees, and remove shoes and hats before entering the main sacred areas.
​Final Verdict
​The Nelligala International Buddhist Centre is a must-visit destination in the Kandy region. It offers a beautiful fusion of modern architectural taste and traditional Buddhist reverence. If you are looking for stunning views, a moment of profound calm, and a chance to experience a newer, rapidly growing pilgrimage site, Nelligala is a spectacular choice that will leave you feeling refreshed and inspired.
​Recommendation: Highly Recommended for its scenery and unique architecture.
Best Time to Visit: Sunrise or Sunset.
Highlights: 360-degree panoramic view, Golden Stupa, and the Statue of God Sumana Saman.

From the moment you begin the ascent, you feel a sense of tranquility. The journey up is part of the experience, with well-paved paths and stairs that are manageable for most. The centerpiece, the magnificent golden stupa, is a sight to behold against the backdrop of the misty Knuckles Mountain Range. The architectural brilliance is a harmonious blend of Sri Lankan, Indian, and Thai styles, with intricate carvings, statues, and pristine white structures at every turn.

What I found most impressive was the immaculate maintenance and the peaceful, organized atmosphere. The entire complex is spotlessly clean, which reflects the deep respect and care the management and devotees have for this sacred space.

Highlights that left me in awe:

· The panoramic view from the upper platforms is simply spectacular, especially in the early morning or late afternoon.
· The giant standing Buddha statue and the various smaller shrines and prayer halls dotted around the hillside.
· The sense of profound peace that permeates the entire area, making it perfect for quiet reflection.

Whether you are a Buddhist pilgrim, a architecture enthusiast, or a traveler seeking a unique and peaceful destination, Nelligala is an absolute must-visit. It’s more than just a temple; it's a masterpiece of faith and artistry. I left feeling rejuvenated and deeply moved. An unforgettable gem in the heart of Sri Lanka.

review_2: 'Tampita viharaya' is a unique image house belonging to the Kandyan era. It is built in stone blocks with the image house resting on top of it in order to prevent moisture, and keep away pests. What used to be a two story image house during it's early built is said to have been destroyed by the Portuguese (16th century) and henceforth was reconstructed to be one story during the reign of king keerthi Sri Rajasinghe (1747-82). Medawala tampita viharaya is considered the oldest among the 200 available in Sri Lanka according to the medawala copper plate. Inside the viharaya one can observe various paintings belonging to the Kandyan era with the centerpiece being a Buddha statue donated from Thailand (Siyam deshaya). On the left side is the depiction of the 'solosmasthana' (16 sacred places) in the order that Sri Lankan Buddhists worship them in (mahiyanganan, nagadeepan, kalyanan, padalanchanan...). This highlights that the seemingly novel 'gatha' which we Sri Lankans use has a history dating back to the kandyan era. On the right side is a depiction of the 'Sri Maha Bodhi' along with 'Sath sathiya' (7 weeks after enlightenment) and various jathaka stories. Preservation work of the temple is being carried out by the funds accumulated by the locals with the guidance of the archaeological department of Sri Lanka. Donations made to the temple would be highly appreciated to preserve this heritage for the future generations to come.

There are very few places this kind of construction can be seen. This temple is very famous for its frescoes which were drawn in Kandiyan era. Kandiyan era frescoes are two dimensional art. No depth is shown during coloring.

review_1:
We had a great experience at the Ceylon Tea Museum. The staff are very friendly and welcoming, and the short guided tour was clear, informative, and not too long. It was really interesting to see the old tea machinery and learn more about the history of tea in Sri Lanka.
The museum is well maintained, and the small tea tasting at the end was a nice touch. The surrounding views are beautiful too, which makes the visit even more enjoyable. Definitely worth a stop if you are in Kandy and want to understand more about Ceylon tea.
review_2:
The Ceylon Tea Museum, located near the center of Kandy, is an insightful and well-preserved attraction dedicated to the history and heritage of Sri Lanka’s world-famous tea industry. Housed in the beautifully restored Hantane Tea Factory, the museum provides a comprehensive look at the evolution of tea cultivation in Sri Lanka, from its introduction by James Taylor in the 19th century to modern-day practices. Visitors can explore antique tea-processing machinery, old photographs, documents, and tools that reveal how tea transformed the island’s economy and culture. The museum also features exhibits on famous tea estates, different types of Ceylon tea, and traditional tea-making techniques.
One of the highlights is the rooftop tea shop, where visitors can enjoy a fresh cup of authentic Ceylon tea while taking in panoramic views of Kandy and the surrounding hills. The museum is well-organized, informative, and engaging for both adults and children. Knowledgeable staff are available to answer questions and provide additional context about the exhibits. While it is not a large museum, every corner offers something to learn or admire, making it a must-visit for history enthusiasts, tea lovers, and anyone curious about Sri Lanka’s tea culture. The museum also offers guided tours for a more immersive experience, adding value to your visit.
review_3:
Visited the Tea Museum on a quiet morning. Was given a great explanation of the history of the museum and then left to explore on my own.
It was low season and there were only two other people there. The museum tells the story of the tea manufacturing industry in a building which housed the factory 100 years ago; it was humble presentation of history and a nice stop away from the bustling centre of Kandy.
Ticket price is 1000 Rupees which also gives you a free cup of tea on the top floor, and 10% off any purchases in the shop. Well worth a visit to explore the history of Ceylon Tea.
review_4:

Location is nearby from the centre, situated on the top of the hill from where you can have an amazing view of tea garden.

Entrance fee is 1000 Rs which include group guided tour and a complementary tea at the restaurant.

The lady ( in 40s)  which was assigned to us for the guidance had absolutely no knowledge about tea or how the process works and why do you go through the process. It seemed she was reading the script to us. We asked her few questions about didn't get any answer.

At the restaurant you can order a THALI TEA for RS1000 which comes with 7 different types of tea.

Overall you can spend 2-3 hours if you have time
review_5: The museum is set in a beautiful location surrounded by views and green. We were guided all through the museum by Padma which was such a treat. We learned so much and really loved seeing all the tea machines. The tasting on the top floor was the best way to end our visit, highly recommend this place!

review_2: Pallekele International Cricket Stadium, located in Kandy, Sri Lanka, is a world-class venue that opened in 2009. With a seating capacity of 35,000, it offers excellent facilities, including modern stands, a media pavilion, and ample parking. The stadium is surrounded by misty mountains, providing a picturesque backdrop for cricket matches. The ground is well-maintained, and the atmosphere during matches is electric, with passionate fans creating a vibrant and supportive environment. Whether you're watching a Test match, ODI, or T20, Pallekele International Cricket Stadium promises an unforgettable cricketing experience.
review_3:
The Pallekele International Cricket Stadium, situated about 15 kilometers east of Kandy, Sri Lanka, is a modern cricket venue that has become an important part of Sri Lanka’s cricketing history. Opened on 27 November 2009, the stadium officially became the 104th Test venue in December 2010, with its first Test match played between Sri Lanka and the West Indies. With a seating capacity of 35,000, it was built in preparation for the 2011 Cricket World Cup, alongside the Hambantota International Cricket Stadium.

Today, Pallekele remains a premier venue for international and domestic matches, with its scenic location and modern facilities making it a favorite for both players and fans. The stadium continues to be an essential part of Sri Lanka's cricketing culture and infrastructure.
review_4:
Pallekele International Cricket Stadium, located in Kandy, Sri Lanka, offers a stunning setting with its backdrop of lush hills, providing a picturesque environment for cricket. It boasts modern facilities and floodlighting, allowing for comfortable viewing and day-night matches.

The well-maintained pitch is known for its balanced nature, fostering competitive play between bat and ball. However, the stadium faces challenges such as frequent weather interruptions due to Kandy's unpredictable climate, accessibility issues for international visitors due to its remote location, and a relatively limited seating capacity of around 35,000, which can restrict spectator numbers for high-demand matches.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Kandy, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
The Kidnapping of Sita by Ravana
Ravana was the king of Lanka and another 9 kingdoms. He was known as Dasis (or Dasa Shirsha) meaning 10 heads, because he had ten crowns, one each for his ten kingdoms.

His sister Surpanakha went to Jambudweepa for some business. Surpanakha chanced upon Rama at his hermitage and became enamored with the handsome prince. Rama being faithful to his wife, Sita, did not respond and asked Surpanakha to approach Lakshmana who was unmarried. Surpanakha, who felt humiliated by this, tried to attack Sita in anger saying Sita was the cause of the men’s contempt for her. Lakshmana then intervened and cut off Surpanakha’s nose.

Surpanakha, terrified and in pain, flew at once to Lanka to seek the protection of Ravana. When Ravana asked his sister for the cause, Surpanakha said that she had seen Sita, a lady of incomparable beauty, and wanted to bring her for Ravana. Ravana resolved to take revenge for the insult his sister had suffered, as well as to get lovely Sita for him self, and set out to abduct Sita and bring her to Lanka.

Ravana, using a golden deer as a decoy, visited Sita when she was alone. In the guise of an old sage, he abducted and brought her to Weragantota in Lanka in his plane, the Pushpaka vimana.

Weragantota means the "place of aircraft landing" in Sinhala. This is the first place Sita Devi was brought to Lankapura (capita city of king Ravana). These jungles are the place where the city of Lankapura once stood. The city had a beautiful palace for Queen Mandothari surrounded by waterfalls, streams and varieties of flora and fauna. Sita Devi was kept at Queen Mandothari’s palace at Lankapura. The place Sita was held captive is called Sita Kotuwa, which means "Sita’s Fort" in Sinhala. It is believed Ravana had an aircraft repair center at Gurulupotha close to Sita Kotuwa. Sita devi was kept in queen Mandothari’s palace until she was moved to Sita Kotuwa and then on to Ashoka Vatika. The remains that are found here are the remnants of later civilizations. In Valmiki's depiction, King Ravana’s vimana resembled a huge peacock. The vimana in Sinhala language means "Dhandu Monara" which is known as "flying peacock," and hence the name Gurulupotha, which means "parts of birds" in Sinhala. This is also called Gavagala.

Seetha Amman Kovil in Nuwara Eliya is a must-visit temple filled with peace and history. It’s believed to be the place where Princess Seetha from the Ramayana was held captive by King Ravana. You can still see the stream where she is said to have bathed and some rock marks believed to be Hanuman’s footprints. The temple has beautiful South Indian architecture and a calm spiritual vibe.

For locals, entry is free, but foreigners may have to get a ticket or pay for special pooja services. It’s about 5 km from Nuwara Eliya town near Hakgala Gardens. The place gets crowded during holidays, so early morning visits are best.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Nuwara Eliya, Sri Lanka.
display_review: If you close your eyes and you open them only after being surreptitiously brought to this site, you would be convinced that you have been brought to the European countryside. While it is difficult to believe, the fact remains that this beautifully designed building is a functional Post Office at Nuwara Eliya in Sri Lanka. It was constructed in 1815 by the erstwhile British rulers and handed over to the Postal Department in 1894. A sight of the building made with red bricks, and the adjoining Clock Tower with the hilly landscape in the background emits obvious romantic vibes. Well, if you want to stay here, let me specify that in 2012, the Post Master’s quarters upstairs was converted into a lodge for tourists.

Sending a postcard from here feels special, and the staff are friendly and helpful. The building's a great example of the town's heritage, and it's a nice spot for a quick photo op. Even if you're not mailing anything, it's worth a visit just to soak up the atmosphere. Plus, it's a great place to grab a few postcards and send some love to friends and family ❤️
review_3:
I noticed tourists milling around outside and stopped to take a look, only to discover that this is the oldest post office in Sri Lanka. The building architecture is charming and full of character, reflecting a nostalgic colonial era with its signature red brick exterior.

Inside, locals still come in to use the postal services, which adds to the authentic atmosphere. I bought stamps for my postcard, which I had purchased in Sigiriya for 100 rupees. In hindsight, I should have bought the postcards here, as the post office sells much nicer ones at about half the price! Postcard stamps are 70 rupees a card.

There is also a small gift shop inside offering souvenirs if you would like a keepsake. The post office still has old equipment on display, such as a traditional gluing counter, which customers can use. I highly recommend stopping by for a visit.

review_4:
Wonderful church with a significant historical value, connected to the colonial history of Ceylon and Nuwara Eliya, a place all tourists better not miss. Now (from Jan 2024) open for public throughout the day (earlier used to be open only from 1500 - 1800 hrs).

It's an Anglican church, considered to be the oldest in the city, consecrated in 1852. It's a cruciform church building (clearly visible in satellite view on Google maps) with a conical shaped wooden roof. The colorful stained glasses is a highlight of the building which are artistic masterpieces. The centenary old pipe organ had been restored and is being currently used. The benches and most of the furniture and fixtures are custom-made from imported wood. Most are preserved in it's original form. The Church is now declared (in 2007) as an archeological protected monument.

There are a number of memorial plaques on the walls as well as the churchyard. These include the plaques for several renowned British governors, bureaucrats, their families, friends and ex-servicemen who lost their lives during WW2. These include - Francis Henry Hamilton (the son of Gavin Hamilton, government agent for revenue & commerce, who built the Hamilton Canal), Lady Carolina Ridgeway (the wife of Sir Joseph West Ridgeway, who was instrumental in building the Lady Ridgeway Hospital in Colombo), family members of Samuel Baker.

Douglas, the caretaker, is very knowledgeable of the historical importance of the church and all it's content. You'll be amazed with all his explanations. The Queen Elizabeth II and Prince Philip had visited the Church in 1954, and said to have gifted two main stained glass windows in remembrance of their visit (Douglas will show you).

Highly recommended for all visitors to Little England.

description: Serene botanical garden featuring lush plantings, including a wide variety of orchids & roses.
display_review:
Beautiful and peaceful place to spend a few hours in Nuwara Eliya. The gardens are well kept, with a huge variety of flowers, ferns, and trees spread across different zones. The cool mountain air and mist make the walk relaxing and scenic, especially in the mornings.

People have to buy tickets at the entrance, and payments can be made by credit card. Seniors get a discount. The ticket price is high to be honest. Parking can be a hassle on busy weekends.

We visited multiple times between April and October and were able to see and enjoy plenty of flora. November to March can be flower less. We went in January once and there were no flowers at all.

The rose garden and orchid house are highlights when they are in bloom. The location under Hakgala Rock gives great views across the valley. There is a cafeteria where you can buy food and drinks, and small shops that sell flower plants and souvenirs.

A few areas could use better maintenance and clearer signs. Overall, it is worth visiting if you enjoy nature and photography. One of the best botanical gardens in Sri Lanka.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Nuwara Eliya, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
Ambagamuwa inscriptions (epigraphs) are considered as one of the main sources of information in Polonnaruwa Era.

According to chronicles the king rested here en route to Sri Pada with his retinue while on pilgrimage.  The inscription has been made on the 36th year of King Vijayabahu I (1070-1110). These are engraved on 2 rocks which are 12 feet 3 inches x 9 feet 3 inches and the the other 9 feet 4 inches x 9 feet 7 inches in size.

These inscriptions describe the defeat of the Tamil invading forces by the King Vijayabahu I and bringing country under one rule, it also describes the work done by the king to the people and donations made to the Sri Pada pilgrims and the Sri Pada.

This inscription is located ab0out 400 meters from the Nawalapitiya – Ginigathhena Road (B319) at a vilage called Sellipigama in the Ambagamuwa town.

Unfortunately there is no archeology department board at the turn off to Sellipigama on the Nawalapitiya road. Either its broken down or never installed. The only indication is a cemented notice board on the ground at the top of the tiny road. This notice lies on the left of the road so that it is visible only if you are traveling from Nawapitiya.

This is a small tared road with most of the tar washed away. Travel about 400 meters along this road. This road will folk in to 3 small roads at about 200 meters and you need to take the left most road.

The inscriptions lies on a small triangular piece of land fenced by the archeological department. A small gate provides access into the rocks with inscriptions. Unfortunately this does not seem to be maintained properly with overgrown weeds surrounding the rocks. There is an open crack on one stone inscription which villagers say may be caused by the spreading roots of a breadfruit tree nearby.
review_1:
Ambagamuwa inscriptions (epigraphs) are considered as one of the main sources of information in Polonnaruwa Era.

According to chronicles the king rested here en route to Sri Pada with his retinue while on pilgrimage.  The inscription has been made on the 36th year of King Vijayabahu I (1070-1110). These are engraved on 2 rocks which are 12 feet 3 inches x 9 feet 3 inches and the the other 9 feet 4 inches x 9 feet 7 inches in size.

These inscriptions describe the defeat of the Tamil invading forces by the King Vijayabahu I and bringing country under one rule, it also describes the work done by the king to the people and donations made to the Sri Pada pilgrims and the Sri Pada.

This inscription is located ab0out 400 meters from the Nawalapitiya – Ginigathhena Road (B319) at a vilage called Sellipigama in the Ambagamuwa town.

Unfortunately there is no archeology department board at the turn off to Sellipigama on the Nawalapitiya road. Either its broken down or never installed. The only indication is a cemented notice board on the ground at the top of the tiny road. This notice lies on the left of the road so that it is visible only if you are traveling from Nawapitiya.

This is a small tared road with most of the tar washed away. Travel about 400 meters along this road. This road will folk in to 3 small roads at about 200 meters and you need to take the left most road.

The inscriptions lies on a small triangular piece of land fenced by the archeological department. A small gate provides access into the rocks with inscriptions. Unfortunately this does not seem to be maintained properly with overgrown weeds surrounding the rocks. There is an open crack on one stone inscription which villagers say may be caused by the spreading roots of a breadfruit tree nearby.
review_2: This is a stone inscription erected by King Vijayabahu the Great. The road from Rajarata to Gilimale, where the pilgrims used to go on pilgrimage to Sripa, was erected in the area where this inscription was erected. The villages mentioned here can still be identified from this area with minor changes. This place is well maintained. The road is narrow. But since there is little traffic, it is not a big problem. There is no special place to park the vehicle.
review_3: This inscription, erected in the 38th year of the reign of King Vijayabahu I, was primarily intended to publicize a village offering made by the king to the Sripada site. The purpose of the Gilimale inscription, erected at the same time, is also the same. Although the Gilimale and Ambagamuwa inscriptions are believed to have been erected with the same purpose, the value of the Ambagamuwa inscription is further enhanced as the Gilimale inscription has been obliterated beyond recognition.
review_4:
Sinhala English Note.
The Ambagamuva inscription is established by King Vijayabahu first or Maha Vijayabahu who ruled the Rajarata Polonnaruwa period.
It includes the liberation of the Rajarata area from the Chola rule and the services rendered there by the king Sripada Vedapuda.

The Ambagamuva inscription is established by King Vijayabahu first or Maha Vijayabahu who ruled the Rajarata Polonnaruwa period.
It includes the liberation of the Rajarata area from the Chola rule and the services rendered there by the king Sripada Vedapuda.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Nuwara Eliya, Sri Lanka.
display_review: Beautiful little station on the hill, filled with nostalgia. There are two platforms and car parking outside. This is one of the main stops of the scenic train route from Kandy to Ella. The slow train tickets have to be validated by the station master who still wears the old style uniform. There is a toilet available on pay per use. The trains are usually late but the station has waiting areas and even a small cafe on the platform for buying snacks and beverages.
review_1: Beautiful little station on the hill, filled with nostalgia. There are two platforms and car parking outside. This is one of the main stops of the scenic train route from Kandy to Ella. The slow train tickets have to be validated by the station master who still wears the old style uniform. There is a toilet available on pay per use. The trains are usually late but the station has waiting areas and even a small cafe on the platform for buying snacks and beverages.
review_2: Beautiful little station surrounded by misty hills and tea plantations. It’s the main stop for Nuwara Eliya, with a cool, old-fashioned charm. The views from the train are stunning, but the station can get crowded during peak hours. Facilities are basic but decent. Be cautious with tuk-tuk drivers outside. Overall, a memorable stop on the scenic train journey through Sri Lanka!
review_3: Nearest railway station to nuwaraeliya.. infront of the station main road, buses are available to nuwaraelia in every 30 minutes. Don't froget to see the ice fall water fall that is 400 meters away from the station.
review_4:
This station is one of the spots for you to hop on/off the scenic train that heads towards Colombo, Ella and Badulla.

The station is fine, it has a cafe, the toilets need some work and the train was about 1 hour delayed.

The train ride itself is beautiful but I'm not sure if it's a must do in Sri Lanka as you can see similar views driving or staying in the hotels in Ella. If you do plan on doing the train, I recommend the second class reserved seats. Also note, a lot of passengers thought the train will go over the Nine Arch Bridge. It does, but you have to keep going past Ella station towards Badulla station. Most people get off at Ella.
review_5: Here i met a beautiful poetry girl. by the way its a beautiful station.

review_1: Very old and renowned temple in Kurunegala. People come in numbers to pray here.
review_2: ancient and historical Temple located in kurunegala district.
review_3: Renowned historic and religious place which is commonly called as "Vilbawa dewalaya" rather than "Vilbawa temple". Dewalaya will be opened on Wednesday and Saturday morning period except poya days. This ancient temple has an annual perahera ceremony as well.
review_4: Ancient temple. People visit here occasionally. Bit crowded in weekends and holidays.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Nuwara Eliya, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
It’s a popular spot for nature lovers, hikers, and those seeking a peaceful escape with a touch of romance. The trail to the waterfall is scenic and relatively short, passing through Pedro Tea Estate, making the journey just as memorable as the destination.

Also it was about 200m easy hike.
review_1:
It’s a popular spot for nature lovers, hikers, and those seeking a peaceful escape with a touch of romance. The trail to the waterfall is scenic and relatively short, passing through Pedro Tea Estate, making the journey just as memorable as the destination.

Also it was about 200m easy hike.
review_2:
Awesome spot. The walk is slightly hard so wouldn’t recommend it for people with knee issues, back issues or poor stamina. The climb is easy to moderate (mostly easy) but does require your to wear footwear with some grip. The climb is divided in 3 stages-

Stage 1: fairly easy, mostly steps - you can see the falls (at least first half of it, the fall)

Stage 2: easy to moderate climb, mostly no steps and its like a hike. U can go close to the falls - but not swim. Swimming is not allowed. But the view is amazing. It took us ~40-50 min to complete stage 2 climb.

Stage 3: optional. Moderate to slightly hard, you can go to the top of the falls. Recommended only for people with good physical fitness and stamina. Its another 30-40 min climb.

Stage 2 is perfect for a beautiful view and in interest of time.
review_3: You anyway have to do a minimum of 200m hike to reach this place but it is not difficult. What we did was to park the car in lovers leaf main road ( coordinates 6.974070215509155, 80.78391208535895) and walk to the waterfall since the road condition beyond that location was not good as per our comfort. Therefore we had to walk a total of about 900m to reach the place but the journey was not that hard. you can follow foot path to reach the waterfall. vehicle parking anyway is not easy to find in the car park areas shown in google map. waterfall is beatiful and the walk is worth considering the experience and the beautiful view
review_4: We got there from center Nanu oya by tuktukfor 609 LKR. Before we visited Victoria parc which has a beautiful landscape and nice flowers. At the exit you pass by the pink post office where you can write and send postcards like in the 19th century . Picking up a Tuktuk with some negotiations will bring you to 2.5 km and 15 min ride to the entrance of the lovers leap waterfall . The climb will take you around 20 mins bit the view is spectacular . There is an easier descent with 500 m walk to the Main Street where taxi pickup is easier . Nanu Oya is a worthy stopover and our high recommendation for a stay is Suriya Guesr hotel with the Ayurvedic Cafe .
review_5:
Lover's Leap Waterfall is a beautiful and peaceful spot just a short drive from Nuwara Eliya town. The walk to the falls takes you through lush tea plantations, making the journey just as scenic as the destination . The waterfall itself is stunning, cascading from around 30 meters high into a quiet, green setting .

The site is tied to a poignant local legend about a prince and a village girl who leapt from this spot to be together forever, which adds a layer of history and mystery to the visit .

The path is relatively easy, but I'd recommend wearing comfortable shoes with good grip as it can be uneven and slippery, especially after rain . It's a great stop to enjoy nature, take in the views, and capture some beautiful photos away from the busier town centers . The best time to visit is in the morning or early afternoon for clear weather.

display_review:
Adams banglow haputale
It appears you may be referring to Adisham Bungalow in Haputale, as there is no prominent attraction named "Adams Bungalow" in the area, but "Adisham" is often misheard or misremembered.

The Adisham Bungalow, also known as Adisham Hall or St. Benedict's Monastery, is a significant colonial-era attraction near Haputale, Sri Lanka.

Here is a summary of the information:

🏰 Adisham Bungalow Overview
History: It was built in 1931 by Sir Thomas Lister Villiers, a prominent English planter and aristocrat, in the Tudor and Jacobean architectural styles, inspired by Leeds Castle in Kent.

Current Use: After Sir Thomas's retirement, the property changed hands and was eventually acquired by the Roman Catholic Church in 1961. It currently houses the Adisham Monastery of Saint Benedict.

Location: It is situated amidst the misty hills and tea plantations, about 3-4 km from Haputale town, offering panoramic views of the Uva valley.

Visitor Access: Parts of the house, including the library and living room, are open to the public on specific days, typically weekends, Poya days (Full Moon days), and public/school holidays, usually from 9:00 AM to 4:30 PM.

🌳 What to See and Do
Architecture: Admire the well-preserved Tudor and Jacobean style architecture, antique furniture, and period fittings.

Gardens: Stroll through the vast, enchanting English-style gardens, which are beautifully maintained.

Monastery Kiosk: A small shop on the premises sells homemade jams, pickles, and coffee liqueurs, often made with produce from the monastery farms.

Views: Enjoy the serene atmosphere and breathtaking views of the surrounding hills and valleys, including the mountain range known as the "Sleeping Warrior."

🛌 Accommodation
While the main Adisham Bungalow is an active monastery and not a typical commercial hotel, some search results indicate:

Limited Retreat Accommodation: The monastery sometimes offers limited accommodation for guests seeking a Christian spiritual retreat in an atmosphere of peace and solitude. Booking this usually requires contacting the monastery directly, often through the Father in charge, sometimes with a letter of recommendation.

Nearby Options: There are several other holiday bungalows and guesthouses in the vicinity of Haputale, such as "Adisham Village Home," which offer standard accommodation for travelers visiting the area.

review_2:
Adisham Bungalow is one of the most charming and peaceful places to visit in Sri Lanka!  Surrounded by beautiful gardens, misty hills, and fresh mountain air, it feels like stepping into a little piece of England in the heart of Haputale. The bungalow is well-preserved with a rich history, and the atmosphere is calm and serene.

It’s a perfect spot to enjoy nature, take in the stunning views, and learn about the heritage of this iconic building. The strawberry jams and fresh produce available here are also delightful. 🍓✨ Highly recommend visiting for anyone who loves history, nature, and quiet beauty! 💚
review_3: Adisham Bungalow is a hidden gem in Haputale. Peaceful gardens, historic stone architecture, and stunning mountain views. The architecture and interior give a glimpse of history, and the gardens are perfect for photos. Only a few rooms are open to the public, and it can get busy on weekends, but overall it’s a wonderful experience. Best visited in the morning when it’s less crowded.Don’t miss the homemade jams at the little shop
review_4: Adisham Bungalow in Haputale is a truly enchanting place, full of history and charm. Surrounded by peaceful tea plantations and fresh mountain air, it feels like stepping into a different world. The stone architecture and beautiful gardens are perfectly preserved, and the interior reflects its rich colonial heritage. The small museum and shop offer interesting insights and delicious homemade products. The staff are friendly and welcoming, making the visit even more pleasant. A must-visit spot for anyone exploring the hill country of Sri Lanka!
review_5: Beautiful place to enjoy nature. Tried their Mixed fruit jam from the shop, it was super fresh and delicious. Now they offer accomodation with food, you have to make a reservation 2 weeks in advance.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Nuwara Eliya, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
A beautiful historic church and cemetery.
We were lucky to get the chance to go inside the church, and it was absolutely lovely. The place also offers a great view of the Castlereigh Reservoir. If you’re visiting Dick Oya, this is definitely a must-visit spot!
review_1:
A beautiful historic church and cemetery.
We were lucky to get the chance to go inside the church, and it was absolutely lovely. The place also offers a great view of the Castlereigh Reservoir. If you’re visiting Dick Oya, this is definitely a must-visit spot!
review_2:
The church is perched on a hilltop overlooking the Castlereagh Reservoir. The view from the church is picturesque.
The church grounds has lots of graves from the 1800s. The stained glass window art, an old bible and the pipe organ inside add to the beauty
review_3:
🌿⛪ Christ Church, Dickoya
Tucked away in the tea country, this little church feels like a doorway to another time. The stone walls whisper stories from the colonial past, while the cool mist outside paints a picture you won’t find anywhere else. It’s not just a church – it’s a moment of calm, framed by rolling hills and endless green.

A visit here is like pausing life for a while – history, faith, and nature blending into one beautiful scene. ✨
review_4:
Beautiful church located in a breathtaking location. The structure of the church and ambience is amazing. Beautiful views of mountains and the Castlereigh reservoir.

Services are on Sundays at 8 am.
review_5:
This Anglican church built during the colonial era has been preserved and well maintained. Originally used by the tea planters and their families. The little graveyard is filled with tombstones of the community. The first British colonial Inspector General of the Ceylon Police, Sir George William Robert Campbell is believed to be buried here.

The highlights are - the beautiful stained glass windows (depicting the highlights of the life of Jesus Christ), a century old pipe organ and the Bible, designer floor tiles, all brought down from the UK. Most of the furniture and fittings are also original. The care taker Brian was helpful and knowledgeable of the historical importance and details of the place. The old pipe organ is currently defunct pending some essential repairs, but they are still searching for a person/company to do the repairs. A vintage Yamaha wind organ is being used temporarily. The efforts by the Church of Ceylon, the vicar and the management to maintain the church in this excellent condition is commendable.

Location, overseeing the Castlereigh Reservoir, is perfect. A place you mustn't miss if you are in/passing the area. A full walkthrough will take approx. 30 minutes and ideal times of visit are mornings or evenings. Be prepared with some currency for a donation, which isn't mandatory but customary.

review_4:
A very beautiful and ancient temple, with a rich cultural significance. The temple is relatively less known to tourists but is quite well known among the local people.

The temple is very close to the city center, with about 5 minutes drive by tuk-tuk. It is well maintained and there is ample parking.

The temple opening hours are 7.30 am to 12.30 pm and 3.30 pm to 6.30 pm.

On Fridays and other festival days, the temple is kept open till 2 pm.
review_5:
A lovely temple of Devi Gayatri and Shiva, with calm and peaceful vibes. The purohitas managing the temple are friendly and informative.

In Pauranika lore, it is recorded as the place where Indrajita (son of Ravana) did tapasya. Maha Samadhi of the Siddha who founded the temple is nearby.

Alongside the existing temple, a huge temple of 108 Shivalingas is under construction as of June 2024.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Nuwara Eliya, Sri Lanka.
display_review: If you close your eyes and you open them only after being surreptitiously brought to this site, you would be convinced that you have been brought to the European countryside. While it is difficult to believe, the fact remains that this beautifully designed building is a functional Post Office at Nuwara Eliya in Sri Lanka. It was constructed in 1815 by the erstwhile British rulers and handed over to the Postal Department in 1894. A sight of the building made with red bricks, and the adjoining Clock Tower with the hilly landscape in the background emits obvious romantic vibes. Well, if you want to stay here, let me specify that in 2012, the Post Master’s quarters upstairs was converted into a lodge for tourists.

review_2: The Nuwara Eliya Post Office is a quaint little spot! 📨🏠 It's like stepping back in time, with its colonial architecture and charming vibes. Sending a postcard from here feels special, and the staff are friendly and helpful. The building's a great example of the town's heritage, and it's a nice spot for a quick photo op. Even if you're not mailing anything, it's worth a visit just to soak up the atmosphere. Plus, it's a great place to grab a few postcards and send some love to friends and family ❤️
review_3:
I noticed tourists milling around outside and stopped to take a look, only to discover that this is the oldest post office in Sri Lanka. The building architecture is charming and full of character, reflecting a nostalgic colonial era with its signature red brick exterior.

Inside, locals still come in to use the postal services, which adds to the authentic atmosphere. I bought stamps for my postcard, which I had purchased in Sigiriya for 100 rupees. In hindsight, I should have bought the postcards here, as the post office sells much nicer ones at about half the price! Postcard stamps are 70 rupees a card.

There is also a small gift shop inside offering souvenirs if you would like a keepsake. The post office still has old equipment on display, such as a traditional gluing counter, which customers can use. I highly recommend stopping by for a visit.
review_4: Nuwara Eliya Post Office is a beautiful historical landmark with stunning colonial-era architecture. Its bright red roof and classic design make it a popular spot for photos. Visiting here gives a glimpse into the town’s history while enjoying a peaceful and charming atmosphere. A must see when exploring Nuwara Eliya.
review_5: A famous old building in Sri Lanka that belongs to the Postal Department. It has a unique architectural design. You can buy postal stamps, souvenirs, postcards, etc, from the shop inside the building. Parking can be found on the nearby roads without a challenge.

description: An ancient and sacred Buddhist temple in Nuwara Eliya, reflecting Sri Lanka's deep religious heritage.
display_review: Hanumanji's temple (visited on 24th Oct 2025), having mythological significance, is located off Kandy-Nuwara Eliya route where you can experience peace and calmness. The temple, with the tallest statue of Hanumanji in Sri Lanka, overlooks the beautiful valley and has a large complex with a cafe and a souvenier shop. It also offers a dedicated vegetarian dining having good taste, so please keep this in mind when in sri lanka and looking for vegetarian option, although we just got a sambhar as jain.
