The Bhakta Hanuman Temple in Sri Lanka — such a peaceful and divine place!
The 40 ft Hanuman statue is truly magnificent and inspiring. The temple surroundings are calm and serene, and the mountain view right in front of the temple adds to its beauty. A must-visit spot for anyone traveling around Ramboda or Nuwara Eliya — it’s a place where you instantly feel peace and positivity.
review_3: One of the biggest temples and tallest Hanuman statue in Sri Lanka. It is situated on a hill overlooking the Kotmale reservoir. Very calm and full of spiritual energy. The main temple is tall building and you have to take stairs to reach the area. Parking is available near the hill. The view to the reservoir is serene and beautiful. It said to be the place where Shri Ram assembled the troops before the final battle of Ramayan. No tickets for entry.
review_4: It is located on a hilltop and it's a windy road trip up. It is a popular stop for travellers on the Ramayana trail. There is long steep stairs from the carpark to the temple and some may find it a hard climb.  Amazing landscape  views from the temple grounds. To enter the temple one must be attired properly including ladies covering their legs. Garments are available for hire before the stairs. Need hat, comfy shoes, water,  moderate fitness to climb.
review_5: The silence and the divine vibrations of this temple is something I’ll cherish for a long time. If in Sri Lanka, this is a must visit irrespective of your religious beliefs. Just walking around this place is enough to experience bliss!

description: Scenic hotel with an upscale restaurant, a British pub & gardens, plus a snooker room.
display_review:
We had an absolutely amazing time here! The service was outstanding, and the food was truly enticing—every detail exceeded our expectations. We especially loved the bonfire, the perfect warmth in the room, and the overall comfort, which made our stay both memorable and delightful.

A special word of appreciation to Mr. Mangalam for his gracious hospitality and attention to detail. The hotel’s vintage charm, beautifully enhanced with Christmas décor throughout, added a magical and festive touch to our experience.

This hotel has the sort of vintage charm many people can appreciate. The building is 150 years old and has served as a hotel for more than 100.
We have stayed here and we felt really welly taken care of.
The food at the restaurant was delicious and very abundant. They catered our room with delicious local stuff.
They have a really old snooker table and two receptionists, mr. Roy and Mr. Kamal taught us the game and played with us. They also have beautiful gardens, a drawing room, a fireplace, etc
We really recommend you stay here when visiting the Sri Lankan hill country
review_3: My husband and I stayed three nights at Jetwing St Andrew’s in November as part of our tour, and we had an absolutely fantastic time. The facilities were outstanding and the service went above and beyond throughout our stay. We thoroughly loved the décor and overall look of the accommodation, and the grounds were just beautiful as well. Everything about this place was amazing — we honestly couldn’t fault it. Truly fantastic accommodation and a highlight of our trip.
review_4:
Very nice hotel the crew is very gracious. The view is amazing.
1 employee in particular made me feel very good and he taught me how to play snooker. It was really fun and it probably wouldn’t have been without him His name is Thivanka🧡
Overall, very nice experience I was just missing air-conditioning but the hotel gave me a fan and it was very sufficient
Highly recommended🤩
review_5:
This is an outstanding heritage property which carries it’s Scottish legacy even today. Nuwara Eliya, the City, is known as Little England. Extending this little more I’d like to call Jetwings St. Andrew’s campus as Mini Scotland.
The maintenance of this 150 years Scottish Legacy is being done superb & definitely this isn’t an easy job. Kudos to the Jetwings St. Andrew’s Management & all the team members for meticulously maintaining the standard of maintenance, hygiene, housekeeping & most importantly the guest treatment to such a great level.
There are only 52 rooms with 2 suites in this property.
Special mention needs to be made about the Front Office Manager Mr. Clive who takes relentless care in the nitty gritty for ensuring guest delight.
Breakfast buffet spread is just awesome with blend of English Breakfast dishes & local Sri Lankan delicacies. Even the choice of fruit basket is full with seasonal choices.
I wish the Jetwings team all the very best.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Nuwara Eliya, Sri Lanka.
display_review: Kotmale Mahaweli Maha Seya is a truly peaceful and spiritual place to visit. The stupa is massive and very beautiful, built as a tribute to the ancient Buddhist heritage of Sri Lanka. The location provides breathtaking views of the surrounding greenery and Mahaweli River, making it a perfect spot for those seeking tranquility. Kothmale Mahaweli Maha Seya is a truly magnificent stupa built in memory of the submerged villages of the Mahaweli Development Project. 🕉️✨ The structure is huge and impressive, standing tall amidst the beautiful hills of Kotmale, surrounded by lush greenery and fresh mountain air.

It is well maintained, and you can really feel the blend of history, culture, and natural beauty here. A must-visit destination for those who love heritage sites and scenic hill-country views.

review_5:
An area that was separate as a country in the past.  There are 4 entrances to Kothmale.  It is Kadadora, Vatadora, Nigamgamdora and Galdora.  Dehadu Kadulla, also known as Kadadora, is one of the entrances.[1]  This entrance is made using stone blocks and sakka stones.  Due to the fact that this is an entrance between Ruhuna and Maya country, taxes have also been levied between the two countries.  Ancient Kotmale belonged to Ruhunu country.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Nuwara Eliya, Sri Lanka.
display_review: Mandaramnuwara is one of the most beautiful and untouched villages I’ve ever seen in Sri Lanka. Surrounded by misty mountains, green tea estates and fresh waterfalls, the scenery is absolutely breathtaking. The weather is cool and peaceful – perfect for relaxing away from the busy city life. Locals are very friendly and helpful. Great place for nature lovers, hiking, photography and exploring hidden gems. Highly recommended if you want a calm and magical getaway.
review_1:
Our time in Mandaram Nuwara felt like stepping into another world — a place where time slows down, the mist dances over the hills, and every breath feels pure and peaceful.

From the moment we arrived, we were  surrounded by kindness and calm. The hotel staff welcomed us with such warmth that  immediately felt at home. The view from our room was breathtaking — endless green tea fields fading into soft white clouds. Each morning, I woke up to the gentle sound of rain and birds, and it was truly magical.

The tea experience was something I’ll never forget. Drinking a fresh cup of Ceylon tea grown right in the surrounding hills, while watching the mist roll through the valley, was pure bliss. The flavor was rich and comforting — you could taste the freshness of the mountain air in every sip.

Every detail at the hotel made the stay special — the cozy atmosphere, the delicious food, and the way the team genuinely cared about making guests happy. It wasn’t just a stay; it felt like being part of a peaceful little world hidden in the clouds.

Mandaram Nuwara will always hold a special place in my heart. It’s the perfect escape for anyone who loves nature, tea, and tranquility. I left with a full heart, a calm mind, and memories I’ll cherish forever.

⭐ Truly a magical and peaceful experience
review_2: Just five stars does not do the justice. It deserves even more. Maybe the route to get to the location is a bit rural and difficult. But it's really worth it. One of the most unspoiled amazing clean environment you can experience. Lots of waterfalls to play in as well. The hotel facilities and the staff was amazing, thoughtfull and supportive too...food was really good as well.
review_3: I had the privilege of staying here for two nights, and it truly exceeded all my expectations. I am honestly at a loss for words—what an incredible property! The staff is amazing, the service exceptional, and the food absolutely delightful. The views are breathtaking, the rooms are next-level, and every amenity you could need is thoughtfully provided. This is a remarkable property in a true gem of a location.
review_4:
Mandaram Nuwara “You can't beat the location of [Tea & experience Hotel]it was so easy to get to all the main attractions. Beyond that, the breakfast buffet was outstanding—so many delicious options. Clean rooms and friendly staff complete the package. We will definitely be back!"
Location is Amazing wow…❤️❤️❤️Manager is Superb
review_5: I had a wonderful experience at Thema Tea & Experience factory. The staff were very friendly and attentive, making me feel truly welcomed. The food was delicious, with a set menu that still offered plenty of choices. The service was excellent, and the environment was calm and elegant. The view was absolutely incredible, adding to the overall atmosphere. The luxurious and spacious rooms made the stay even more comfortable. I really enjoyed the unique tea leaf plucking to cup experience. recommended! 🌟

description: A historical fortification in Matale, serving as a testament to the colonial history of Sri Lanka.
display_review: Extraordinary wonder of nature, shaped by humans. Spectacular views from the top, climbing up is an adventure by itself.  Our visit took 1h-1.5h. Beware of "Sri Lanka Guide Association" guys, you probably don't want to spend 3h with them, especially at their rates.
review_1: Extraordinary wonder of nature, shaped by humans. Spectacular views from the top, climbing up is an adventure by itself.  Our visit took 1h-1.5h. Beware of "Sri Lanka Guide Association" guys, you probably don't want to spend 3h with them, especially at their rates.
review_2:
Be ready to climb 1200 steps to reach the former residence of King Kassapa. There used to be luxurious gardens there, but now there are ruins and simply a nice view. :)
On the way back, be sure to stop by to see the 1500 year-old frescoes. Therefore, don't return the same way you came up, but turn left. The ascent and descent will take you 2-3 hours, including all the accompanying activities. Don't forget sunscreen and water. Entry is $35, which, in my opinion, isn't a fair price for such an activity. But it's still worth seeing this UNESCO World Heritage site :)
review_3:
What a fantastic place.
I visited in October so during the off season which was a blessing " absolutely no crowds "
The price may seem a little steep but by the time you've experienced it,  that'll seem pretty insignificant.
The views are amazing both from up ontop and throughout the gardens,  even before you enter stop to appreciate the manicured landscape and surrounding moat.
review_4:
Sigiriya Lion Rock is easily one of the most extraordinary places I’ve visited. The moment you enter the site, you’re surrounded by beautifully landscaped gardens that were centuries ahead of their time. The climb is a journey in itself—every level reveals something new, from ancient stairways carved into the rock to the world-famous frescoes that still retain their vibrant colors despite being over a thousand years old. The engineering brilliance is unbelievable.
As you climb higher, the massive lion’s paws carved into the stone create a sense of awe, hinting at the grandeur this fortress once held. And when you finally reach the top, the view takes your breath away. Looking out over endless greenery and quiet villages feels like stepping back into a forgotten kingdom. Sigiriya is not just a tourist attraction; it’s a cultural treasure that blends history, architecture, art, and nature in the most breathtaking way.
review_5:
It’s quite a trek to the top of the rock, and it’s not for the faint hearted - but if you can persevere, the views and the experience once you get to the top, are definitely worth the knee trembling moments.
The actual climb up is around 1200 steps, so can be tough going if you’re not overly fit, take water with you - although there’s a refill station on route. Once you get to the top, it’s absolutely amazing to see this incredible fort that was built 1600 years ago - this is right up there with Machu Picchu (built 600 years ago) but with engineering way before its time!

Super religious place. A sacred and peaceful experience.
Rangiri Dambulu Cave Temple is a deeply spiritual and religious place. There is a short climb to reach the temple, but it is well worth the effort. The atmosphere is extremely calm and serene, offering a sense of peace and mindfulness. A must-visit cultural and spiritual landmark in Sri Lanka.
review_3: If you enjoy visiting temples, I highly recommend going here. There is a 30-meter-tall golden Buddha statue. After seeing the Buddha statue, you need to climb a mountain with about 300 steps to reach the main temple area. There, you can see smaller Buddha statues and observe Buddhist ceremonies and rituals. Along the way, you can also enjoy a full view of Dambulla city and witness a beautiful sunset.
review_4: a unique Buddhist temple located inside a natural cave. To reach it, you need to walk up a series of steps, so it can be a bit challenging for people who have difficulty walking—but it’s absolutely worth the effort. Inside, you’ll find fascinating wall paintings and statues, and the atmosphere of the temple is very peaceful and calming. Overall, it’s a special place with a great spiritual feeling and definitely worth visiting if you’re in Sri Lanka.
review_5:
Visiting the Dambulla Cave Temple was one of the highlights of my trip through Sri Lanka. The site is absolutely breathtaking — a peaceful blend of history, spirituality, and art. The caves are filled with stunning Buddha statues and colorful frescoes that have been preserved for centuries, and it’s incredible to see how well they’ve lasted.

The short climb up the hill is totally worth it — not only for the caves themselves but also for the amazing panoramic view from the top. The atmosphere feels calm and sacred, and even with other visitors around, there’s a real sense of tranquility.

Make sure to bring some water, remove your shoes before entering the temple, and take your time exploring each cave — every one tells a different story.

Highly recommend this place to anyone interested in culture, history, or simply a peaceful moment surrounded by beauty.

description: Surrounded by rocks, this historical Buddhist temple features caves containing religious murals.
display_review:
The place where the Tripitaka was first transcribed. The rock cave where this was done is conserved and displayed inside the temple premises. A museum, and a mountain top statue you can reach after a 2km walk through an ascending road are the other attractions here apart from the general temple components.
There wasn't much of a crowd on the typical Saturday morning we visited and parking space was quite sufficient. However, the situation can be different on long weekends or poya days.

review_3: The temple holds immense cultural and religious value—it’s where the Tripitaka was first transcribed onto palm leaves in Sri Lanka, making it a foundational site in Buddhist history.  ￼ ￼
review_4:
Aluvihara Temple, situated in Matale, Sri Lanka, is one of the most historically and spiritually significant Buddhist temples in the country. This sacred site holds a unique place in Buddhist history due to its role in preserving the teachings of the Buddha. It is most renowned for being the location where the Tripitaka and its commentaries (Atthakatha) were committed to writing on ola (palm) leaves for the first time in history.

During the 1st century BCE, Sri Lanka faced a period of great unrest due to South Indian invasions and internal conflicts. King Walagamba, who ruled during this time, had to face constant threats to the country’s stability. With wars, famine, and political disturbances spreading across the island, there was a real danger that the oral tradition of preserving the Buddha’s teachings might disappear. Until then, the Dhamma — including the Vinaya Pitaka (rules of discipline), Sutta Pitaka (sermons), and Abhidhamma Pitaka (philosophical teachings) — had been passed down orally by generations of monks.

Realizing the possibility of this sacred knowledge being lost, the Buddhist monastic community decided to write down the teachings to ensure their survival. Under the guidance of Ven. Mahānāma Thero, the Tripitaka and the Atthakatha were inscribed onto ola leaves at the Aluvihara rock cave complex. This monumental effort not only safeguarded the teachings of the Buddha but also marked one of the most significant moments in the history of Theravāda Buddhism.

The Aluvihara temple complex is built among large rock formations, which give it a serene and secluded atmosphere, ideal for meditation and spiritual practices. The caves where the scriptures were written can still be visited today. These caves also feature ancient paintings, statues of the Buddha, and various depictions of Buddhist teachings and history. The temple’s surroundings, set amidst natural rocky landscapes and greenery, create an environment of peace and reverence.

Today, Aluvihara remains an important pilgrimage site for Buddhists as well as a major historical attraction for visitors from around the world. It serves as a reminder of the resilience and dedication of the ancient monastic community, who ensured the preservation of Buddhism during a time of great adversity. The temple continues to be active, with resident monks conducting daily rituals, Bodhi Pujas, and other religious ceremonies that keep the traditions alive.

Aluvihara Temple is not only a place of worship but also a monument to the intellectual and spiritual determination of the Buddhist monks of ancient Sri Lanka. It stands as a symbol of the island’s rich cultural heritage and its lasting contributions to the preservation and spread of Buddhist teachings
review_5: Birthplace of the Written Tripitaka :-  Its most famous distinction is being the place where the Pāli Canon (Tripitaka), the sacred scriptures of Theravada Buddhism, was first committed to writing on ola (palm) leaves. This monumental task was undertaken by 500 scholarly monks during the 1st century BC, under the reign of King Walagamba, due to fears that the oral tradition of the doctrines might be lost amidst famine and invasions. This act was crucial for the preservation and dissemination of Buddhist teachings.

description: Stone remains of an 8th-10th century Hindu Dravidian style temple & Sri Lanka's center point.
display_review: Built around the 6th century AD, this temple complex is in accordance with the Pallava culture of India. There is evidence that the temple complex has been nourished by the influence of Mahayana Buddhism and Tamil culture. Located in a very beautiful environment, this temple was submerged in the water of the Bowathenna reservoir and was brought to its present location by the current flow. There is also a belief that this place is the center of Sri Lanka. This place is a very valuable archaeological site. The Department of Archaeology maintains a museum at this place and it is a place with parking facilities. There is no entry fee.

Located in a very beautiful environment, this temple was submerged in the water of the Bowathenna reservoir and was brought to its present location by the current flow. review_2: Nalanda Gedige is a hidden gem — ancient, mysterious and beautifully preserved. The mix of Dravidian / Pallava architecture with Buddhist aesthetics makes it unlike anything else I’ve seen in Sri Lanka. Walking through the stone mandapa, seeing the carvings, the moonstone and the statue of Kuvera — every detail speaks of craftsmanship. It’s also fascinating that the whole temple was relocated when the reservoir threatened it, yet it still retains its aura. Surroundings are peaceful, perfect for reflection, and the journey there through rural countryside adds to the charm. Strongly recommend for anyone who loves history, architecture or just looking for somewhere serene to visit.
review_3:
A peaceful and unique historic site!

Nalanda Gedige is a hidden gem blending Hindu and Buddhist architecture, believed to be the center point of Sri Lanka. The word “Gedige” means a structure built entirely with interlocking stone blocks—no mortar used. However this structure has been entirely shifted to a higher ground when the near by tank was constructing.

The site is well maintained, with enough parking and no entry ticket required. The road is in good condition and accessible by any car. A quiet, scenic spot perfect for a short cultural visit. Highly recommended for a nice short visit!
review_4:
Very beautiful place with important history combining Theravada Buddhism, Hinduism and Mahayana Buddhism. It’s one temple with a stupa and small museum. The security guard was very helpful in showing the place. No need to buy tickets here. Little tourists come here. Nearby there is a lake. I could walk in the village, people there are friendly.
I just would prefer not to come on rainy day because one need to walk barefoot in the mud to see the temple and there is no way how to clean the feet there after that.
review_5:
Interesting historic site, located at the center point of Sri Lanka.
For some unknown reason, local tour guides bqrely seem to take tourists here, although it's right on the way between Kandy and Dambulla.
Free entrance, well worth the stop.

description: Ancient Buddhist temple built on a huge rock, a popular hike for history & panoramic views.
display_review: I was so excited to visit the temple and never expected the journey to be this long. The trail wound steeply through the forest, each step feeling heavier as the climb went on. It was a challenging hike, and by the time we reached the top, we were out of breath and covered in sweat. But the moment we saw the panoramic view — mountains stretching endlessly, the temple standing gracefully against the sky, and even the majestic Sigiriya visible in the far distance — every bit of exhaustion melted away. The view from the top truly did not disappoint.

review_2: If you want the budget view of the Lion Rock in Sigiriya, this is the place to go. The entry was about 1000LKR for 1 foreign adult whereas Lion Rock is about 7000LKR. This helps the maintain the place with lighting and other amenities. There is a toilet near the parking along with some condiment stores for refreshments. There’s a temple at the start of the flight of stairs to the top which will need you to be fully covered near your shoulders and knees. You get a Sarong (large shawl) to use, not sure if you have to rent because I didn’t use it. There are stairs of flight almost until 80% of the way and the rest is pure nature rock climb. You need to be fit and willing to do the rest 20% but the view is definitely worth it. Shoes, water bottle, shades, cap and some energy boosters are definitely recommended. Won’t recommend it during monsoon or rains.
review_3:
Great sunrise hike. Pick up was 5am.
Not too challenging, took us about 20-25 mins to reach the top, but you will need some level of fitness.
Cost: 1000 ($5 AUD)
Clothing: on your way down you can visit the temples, so you will need to cover your shoulders and knees.
It's a must do in Sigiriya.
🇦🇺
review_4:
Awesome and challenging hike (30m to 1 hr depending on fitness and volume of people. Views are spectacular. Clambering over rocks is tons of fun, but you need to be fit and confident.

1000 rupees for an adult and 500 for kids, much better value than Sigiriya ($35 USD for adults and $20 for kids).
review_5:
Entrance: 1000 LRK.
There are stairs and lights along most of the way. The last 50-60 meters are a bit rocky, but if you're in reasonably good shape, it’s no problem.
The view at the top is stunning, with a large plateau to enjoy. Unfortunately, we didn’t get a perfect sunrise, but it was still a great experience.

Bring water and wear proper shoes (sneakers or similar).

The only downside? Trash.
In Norway, we have a mindset called “leave no trace.” Many here clearly have something to learn. Plastic takes decades to decompose, and honestly, who do you think will clean up after you? The least we can do as responsible tourists and visitors is to leave the mountain as beautiful as we found it. So pack out your trash. Don’t toss it between rocks or leave it lying around like a lazy slob.

review_1:
Visiting the Arulmigu Sri Muthumariamman Thevasthanam in Matale was a truly memorable experience even though my visit took place in August when the temple was undergoing major restoration. The entire kovil was covered for repairs and I couldn’t enter through the main kovil vasal as it was temporarily closed for safety. Despite this the temple’s beauty and grandeur were still evident beneath the scaffolding. We went there while visiting my husband’s friend’s home in Matale and they kindly took us to the temple knowing how much I appreciate visiting places of devotion and history.

Even though the kovil is not large it holds a majestic and powerful presence that reflects its deep spiritual and cultural importance. The people at the temple were incredibly kind and welcoming and they shared a lot of details about the restoration and upcoming ceremonies. They explained that the Enneykkappu (எண்ணெய்க்காப்பு) will be held around November 26th to 28th and the Maha Kumbabishekam (மஹா கும்பாபிஷேகம்) will take place on November 30th 2025. Listening to them talk about the rituals made me feel the strong devotion and pride the community holds for this sacred site. Even though most parts were covered I could still see the outlines and carvings that showed how magnificent the temple truly is.

The atmosphere around the kovil was calm and peaceful with devotees still coming to offer prayers and light lamps even during the renovation. The devotion and faith that surround this place are what make it so special. The sound of bells prayers and temple birds created a tranquil and spiritual environment that felt timeless. I could imagine how beautiful it must look once everything is restored with the colorful gopuram standing tall and vibrant again.

I would love to visit again during the Enneykkappu or Kumbabishekam ceremonies or perhaps after the restoration is fully completed. It would be wonderful to witness the temple in all its renewed glory. Even though the kovil is temporarily marked as closed on maps you can still visit the area and view the temple from outside, it’s truly worth the visit. The kovil remains as majestic as ever and continues to carry the grace and devotion of generations.

- The entrance fee for Arulmigu Sri Muthumariamman Thevasthanam in Matale appears to be approximately LKR 300 per person.

It’s worth noting that fees and policies can change. I recommend checking with the temple directly on arrival for the latest information.
review_2: Amazing Hindu temple. The intricacies of the deities were so enthralling and made it easy to get immersed in the whole building. We arrived during a festive time so were unable to enter the actual inside of the temple but the outside easily made up for that. Price was 250 slr which you paid at the foreigner window. Locals pay a lot less. No shoes inside as per all temple protocol and wear appropriate clothing. The colours are also another highlight with the Hindu faith. Do not miss this temple if you are in the area.
review_3: Interesting place, but currently under renovation. You can still visit (500 Rps for foreigners), but the outer part ist partly hidden. Inside you can see how they are currently painting everything fresh.
review_4: The Sri Muthumariamman Temple in Matale is one of Sri Lanka's most prominent Hindu temples, revered for its spiritual significance and stunning Dravidian architecture. Dedicated to Goddess Mariamman, the deity of rain and fertility, the temple plays a central role in the religious life of the region's Tamil Hindu community. Its highlight is the annual Theru Festival, held during February or March, where elaborately decorated chariots carry sacred statues of deities in a grand procession through the streets. This vibrant celebration draws thousands of devotees and tourists, symbolizing devotion, cultural pride, and communal unity, while showcasing the temple’s role as both a spiritual sanctuary and cultural landmark.
review_5: This vibrant and colorful Hindu temple is easy to spot along the main road in Matale town. Visitors can walk around the grounds, though it’s unclear if entry inside is allowed. From the southern side, you can catch a glimpse of the interior through the gated entrance. The northern tower is especially impressive, adorned with intricate carvings of Hindu deities—said to number over a thousand. Definitely worth a stop if you’re passing through!

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Matale, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
This Proto-historic Megalithic Burial Site, located near Dambulla, Sri Lanka, is a fascinating and historically rich archaeological site dating back over 2,700 years. This site provides remarkable insight into the lives, rituals, and burial practices of Sri Lanka’s early Iron Age communities. Thoughtfully maintained and well-signposted, the site offers an educational experience that is both immersive and reflective.

The burial ground, showcasing distinct chamber tombs made with stone slabs, reveals advanced burial techniques and social organization. The accompanying signage gives clear explanations in Sinhala, Tamil, and English, highlighting that over 42 burial sites have been excavated. Notably, the site distinguishes between urn burials and non-urn burials—some containing human remains along with grave goods like pottery, beads, and iron tools. This suggests the community had well-developed trade links and a structured society.

Visitors are required to purchase an entrance ticket, which is reasonably priced. Ample parking is available on-site, making it convenient even for group or family visits. The site is clean, quiet, and well-organized, with maintained pathways and shaded resting areas. One of the highlights is the availability of knowledgeable guides stationed at the site who are eager to explain the significance and history of the tombs. Their insights greatly enrich the experience, particularly for those unfamiliar with proto-historic South Asian archaeology.

The open-air layout and the rustic structures over the excavation areas provide a real sense of being close to the past, as you view the burial chambers exactly where they were discovered. Whether you are a history enthusiast, a student, or a curious traveler, Ibbankatuwa is a must-visit cultural site that offers a window into an ancient civilization hidden in plain sight.
review_1:
Pre-historic Burial Site – Well preserved and maintained
We visited the little-known Ibbankatuwa megalithic tomb site situated five kilometres from Dambulla along Dambulla – Kurunegala Road.  The site is about half a kilometre down the road next to the easily visible signboard.  After purchasing our tickets we walked along a well-maintained fence made with wooden posts to the first of the three burial sites that had been excavated so far.  A guide explained that these excavated remain date back 500 BC as proved by carbon dating techniques.
Megalithic means made up with stone. The excavations show clusters of tombs, with each tomb surrounded by four stone slabs covered with another stone slab on top.  Some of these tombs had contained the personal belongings of the dead person such as clay pots and dishes, necklaces and beads.  Some of the pottery articles are left within or around the tombs just as they had been found during archaeological excavations.
The guide explained that there had been two distinct customs of burial.  Remains being placed in urns and interred or ashes interred in pots.
The burial sites are well presented, neat and clean, surrounded by neatly made up fences with wooden posts.  The site is in the middle of the village with villagers houses nearby beyond the fence.  The preservation efforts are still on-going at the third site.
The offices and the site is open from 8.00 am to 5.00 pm.

Barani found from this excavation belongs to two main categories (Burial type) and they are named Shila Manjusa (Stone cist) and Barani (Clay urn) Burial. Also, they are classified into 12 sub-types according to the characteristics of burial architecture, nature of construction, content burial materials and burial urns.

The stone Manjusa tombs were of various sizes, oblong and square in shape. Also, there are two types of burials, without stones and with stones. Barani Burials are of several types. That is, barani burials with a lid and burials with a stone on the surface are called barani. Out of the 47 baranis found in the excavation, 21 of them were earthen vessels containing the ashes of the dead.
Tools made of copper and iron metal and beads of various materials and shapes, terracotta designs, necklaces, bracelets, earrings, etc. could also be identified here, and the remains of jewelry made of special beads typical of the South Indian region such as carnelian, Agade are among the special artifacts found there. is

A large number of ancient beads buried in the tombs were discovered. It is said that these beads were found at the time of excavation as well as at the time of removing the soil from the womb and pouring dry and wet. It is said that the beads were made of clear quarts and carnilean and terra-cotta beads were also found.
The Ibbankatua Cemetery is the largest of the prehistoric burial grounds found in Sri Lanka. The human settlement found in Sri Lanka belonging to the Early Iron Age and its associated burial ground can be named as the settlement site called Polwatta found near Ibbankattuwa. The remains of a coconut grove settlement can be discerned above the Ibbankattuwa lake, parallel to the Ibbankatwa cemetery. It is thought that the dead bodies of the people in that settlement may have been buried here. Although burials believed to belong to this period have been found in Sigiriya and Yatigalpotta areas, the special feature of this is that the mahasila burial in Ibbankattu is very large.

The oldest evidences related to prehistoric culture (ie: 1000 BC, 3000 years ago today) are recorded as including Anuradhapura. Dr. Shiran Deraniyagala is of the opinion that this culture dates back as far as 1000 BC according to radiometric dating and Oxcal chronology. Also, the oldest chronology related to megalithic burials (800 BC) has now been found in Kokebe area. The information related to the iron and copper industry as well as the gold industry in the central Yanoya Valley. P. It is very important to get by around 800.

Its environment was formed in the prehistoric period around the central Yanoya valley as a beautiful land surrounded by a secluded mountain range. Therefore, it can be identified that there are many pre-historic settlements in association with the environment. Gurugalhinna, Dibulwewa, KokEbe, Rambewa, Wadigawewa, Tammannagodella, Parangiyawadiya etc. are the settlements that were born based on the ecosystem. It is said that due to such isolated mountains or mounds, a natural depression has been created in that environment and these depressions become water retention areas with the activation of the North-East Monsoon. Professor Sudarshan Seneviratnean is of the opinion that during the investigation of the location of settlements in the prehistoric period, those settlements were located in places with such landslides. He points out that these settlements can be identified from places such as Ibbankatua, Idamoruluwawewa, Pomparippua etc.
Although such cemeteries are found in abundance in various parts of the island, Ibbankatua Mahashila cemetery is a strong factor that depicts one aspect of the past human society. According to this information, it is confirmed that in the past, Sri Lankans were a human group that claimed a culture with advanced technology in the prehistoric era.

**** Matale Rebellion (1848) ***
By 1848, the Sri Lankans were fed up with British rule. The people were burdened with a large number of taxes such as body tax, coconut tax, toll tax, dog tax, cart tax, stamp duty, etc. A petition was prepared and sent to Governor Torrington to withdraw these taxes, but he rejected it. The people, who were affected by this, protested against the British. The wave of protests that began in this way spread throughout the country as a rebellion. It was led by Gongalegoda Banda and Puranappu. Many unknown heroes including Kudahapola Thero supported it.

Later, the British were able to successfully suppress the rebellion. Gongalegoda Banda, who was arrested on 27 November 1848, was given 100 lashes on 3 May 1849 and sent back to Malacca. Weera Puranappu was executed on 8 August 1848 and Kudahapola Thero on 26 August 1848. Many other heroes were also executed by the British and others were deported. The British later realized that the tyranny of Governor Torrington had caused this rebellion. As a result of the rebellion, oppressive taxes were abolished and measures were taken to protect the Temple of the Tooth.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Matale, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
A Hidden Gem – Arankele Buddhist Monastery Archaeological Site

Arankele is an incredible hidden treasure, showcasing unbelievable ancient stonework and architectural brilliance. This Buddhist monastery archaeological site is rich in history, featuring intricately carved pathways, meditation platforms, and ancient ruins that reflect the craftsmanship of centuries past.

The serene and peaceful environment makes it an ideal place for those interested in history, archaeology, and Buddhism. Walking through the site offers a glimpse into the spiritual and architectural heritage of Sri Lanka.
review_1:
Mahamevnawa Buddhist Monastery in Matale is a place of peace, serenity, and spiritual enrichment. From the moment you enter, you’re greeted by a calming, sacred atmosphere that feels worlds away from the rush of daily life. The monastery is beautifully maintained, with lush greenery, carefully tended gardens, and well-kept paths that encourage quiet reflection. It's a perfect setting to meditate, relax, and experience the true essence of Buddhism.

The monks here are welcoming and devoted to helping visitors understand the teachings of the Buddha. They are happy to answer questions, share insights, or even offer guidance on meditation practices. Unlike some more crowded religious sites, the Mahamevnawa Monastery provides an authentic, peaceful experience where you can connect with your spirituality in a more personal way.

The architecture of the monastery is simple yet beautiful, and the statues and carvings depicting the life of the Buddha add to the sense of tranquility. Each structure and statue feels meaningful, as if placed there with deep respect for the teachings they represent. The quiet surroundings make it an ideal location to learn about Buddhism, with clear signs and texts available to help guide you.

Whether you’re looking to learn more about Buddhism, seeking a spiritual retreat, or simply want to enjoy the serene surroundings, Mahamevnawa Monastery in Matale is truly special. The sense of compassion, humility, and kindness is evident in every part of the monastery and makes for a humbling experience. Visiting here offers a genuine chance to pause, reflect, and find inner peace. Highly recommended for anyone traveling in the Matale area, regardless of faith—this is a place to reconnect with yourself and the beauty of Sri Lankan culture.

description: Mountainous area centered on the Knuckles massif, known for waterfalls, hiking trails & birdlife.
display_review:
The Knuckles Mountain Range also called the Knuckles Massif; a bio-diversity hotspot located in the districts of Kandy and Matale in the Central Province of Sri Lanka. Situated at a height of over 3000 ft above sea level, covering a land area of approximately 21 ha it consists of five peaks; namely Kirigalpottha, Gombaniya, Knuckles, Koboneelagala and Dotulugala which form the shape of the knuckles in a clenched fist. The area situated above 1500 m was declared a climatic reserve in 1873 and a conservation forest in 2000, later a National Man & Biosphere Reserve and a World Heritage Site in 2009. It is today considered as one of South Asia’s important sites for the conservation of montane tropical forest habitats.

This picturesque mist laden mountain range is where the Knuckles Forest Reserve also known as the Knuckles Conservation forest is found. It is an UNESCO designated World Heritage Site that comes under the Central Highlands of Sri Lanka’s World Heritage Property, one of the two World Heritage Natural Properties in Sri Lanka.

The climate here varies from hot to very wet and cold with some higher elevations reporting ground frost during January – March with strong winds at most other times. The vegetation in the valleys and foothills consist mainly of semi evergreen vegetation with large trees, the upper slopes contain tropical montane humid evergreen rain forest and the upper levels possess cloud forests; a moist forest with a low-level cloud cover where various endemic species of ferns, orchids, lichens and mosses grow profusely. The other types of unique vegetation found here includes the distinctively beautiful grasslands called “Pitawala Patana”, the picturesque Riverine forests alongside the rivers and waterways, the Scrublands and the Pygmy forests with its twisted and gnarled plant life that grow up to 1-2 m from ground level.

The Knuckles massif separates itself from the central hills by the Dumbara Valley; a deep gorge. There are several trails to the Knuckles Mountain range, each one different and exclusive, making it ideal for hiking, trekking, waterfall hunting, bird watching and certainly a must place for some scenic photography. Visitors can travel on three routes from Kandy via Rattota, via Wattegama and via Teldeniya.

The Knuckles Forest reserve is considered a vital water catchment with many of its streams feeding important downstream rivers such as the gigantic Mahaweli River (the country’s longest), the Heen River, Maha Oya, Hasalaka Oya and the Theligamu Oya.

The Forest Reserve has recorded up of 1033 plant species belonging to 141 families of which 15% are endemic. In addition there are 128 bird species of which 17 are endemic; some of them are the Black eagle, Pale billed flower pecker, Barbets, Lorikeets, Egrets, Herons, Bulbuls, Babblers, Kingfishers and Wood peckers. The 31 mammal species include the Wild boar, Black-naped hare, Mouse deer, Sambar deer, Elephant, Wild buffalo, Monkey, Squirrel, Civet cat, Golden palm cat, Loris, Jackal, Mongoose, Bandicoot and Porcupine. The 20 amphibians’ species include the Kirthisingha’s Rock Frog and the Leaf nosed lizard, 15 species of fish include the Phillips Garra, Blotched filamented barb and Martenstyn barb. There are 60 species of butterflies, with two endemic species the Birdwing and the Blue Mormon. There are also 17 species of mollusks and 53 species of reptiles.

Pimburaththewa Lake, also known as Pimburattewa Tank, is one of Sri Lanka's most popular tourist spots. Polonnaruwa District's Aralaganwila has Pimburaththewa Lake.

It is well-liked by the locals as a swimming location. Beautiful vistas of the Polonnaruwa district's lush green trees surround the lovely lake. Pimburaththewa Lake is the perfect location to take a bath if you're visiting Polonnaruwa.

The Pimburaththewa Tank is primarily recognized by its lake's water spill design. The water spill is made up of a lot of triangles. There are more than five water spills with triangle designs. It is a must-see location when in Polonnaruwa. Although it is not a waterfall like the one in Polonnaruwa, it appears to be a collection of waterfalls.

If you plan to visit Pimburaththewa Tank, you should exercise caution. Elephants frequently wander the region in search of water. Because elephants frequently emerge in the evening, exercise caution when bathing at this lake.

The beauty of this is also accessible during the wet season. However, it's important to use caution when bathing in the rainy season. One of the best places to see the sunset is at Pimburaththewa Lake. Therefore, if you're in Polonnaruwa, try to have fun.

review_4:
Aluvihara Temple, situated in Matale, Sri Lanka, is one of the most historically and spiritually significant Buddhist temples in the country. This sacred site holds a unique place in Buddhist history due to its role in preserving the teachings of the Buddha. It is most renowned for being the location where the Tripitaka and its commentaries (Atthakatha) were committed to writing on ola (palm) leaves for the first time in history.

During the 1st century BCE, Sri Lanka faced a period of great unrest due to South Indian invasions and internal conflicts. King Walagamba, who ruled during this time, had to face constant threats to the country’s stability. With wars, famine, and political disturbances spreading across the island, there was a real danger that the oral tradition of preserving the Buddha’s teachings might disappear. Until then, the Dhamma — including the Vinaya Pitaka (rules of discipline), Sutta Pitaka (sermons), and Abhidhamma Pitaka (philosophical teachings) — had been passed down orally by generations of monks.

Realizing the possibility of this sacred knowledge being lost, the Buddhist monastic community decided to write down the teachings to ensure their survival. Under the guidance of Ven. Mahānāma Thero, the Tripitaka and the Atthakatha were inscribed onto ola leaves at the Aluvihara rock cave complex. This monumental effort not only safeguarded the teachings of the Buddha but also marked one of the most significant moments in the history of Theravāda Buddhism.

The Aluvihara temple complex is built among large rock formations, which give it a serene and secluded atmosphere, ideal for meditation and spiritual practices. The caves where the scriptures were written can still be visited today. These caves also feature ancient paintings, statues of the Buddha, and various depictions of Buddhist teachings and history. The temple’s surroundings, set amidst natural rocky landscapes and greenery, create an environment of peace and reverence.

Today, Aluvihara remains an important pilgrimage site for Buddhists as well as a major historical attraction for visitors from around the world. It serves as a reminder of the resilience and dedication of the ancient monastic community, who ensured the preservation of Buddhism during a time of great adversity. The temple continues to be active, with resident monks conducting daily rituals, Bodhi Pujas, and other religious ceremonies that keep the traditions alive.

Aluvihara Temple is not only a place of worship but also a monument to the intellectual and spiritual determination of the Buddhist monks of ancient Sri Lanka. It stands as a symbol of the island’s rich cultural heritage and its lasting contributions to the preservation and spread of Buddhist teachings
review_5: Birthplace of the Written Tripitaka :-  Its most famous distinction is being the place where the Pāli Canon (Tripitaka), the sacred scriptures of Theravada Buddhism, was first committed to writing on ola (palm) leaves. This monumental task was undertaken by 500 scholarly monks during the 1st century BC, under the reign of King Walagamba, due to fears that the oral tradition of the doctrines might be lost amidst famine and invasions. This act was crucial for the preservation and dissemination of Buddhist teachings.

display_review:
This magnificent waterfall is situated close to Rattota town in Matale District. Take Matale – Ilukumbura road to the Bamarakiri Falls bend, which is 1km past Rattota town, where the fall is to be found. The Bambarakiri Falls is 18km from Matale town. The Bambarakiri Falls is located approximately 40km away from our Amaya Hunas Falls, one of the most popular honeymoon hotels in Sri Lanka.

The fall is set amidst the mesmerizing greenery of the Knuckles mountain range. Bambarakiri Ella is a fall of 10m. The fall can be viewed in three parts. Different varieties of butterflies and birds come to the area of the falls mostly in clear mornings making the view even more charming.

A very old-fashioned suspension bridge extends over the falls adding an enchanting touch to the already beautiful scene.

Gallons of water come crashing down to earth at a high speed. The rock pool at the base of the waterfall is safe for a dip during the dry summer, but can be very dangerous during heavy rainy season.

For those who yearn for more adventure, there is a steep slippery climb of 5km, to reach the top of the waterfall. But one should bear in mind that a slight mistake will result in grave injury.

Although the climb is not easy and should be done with care, the views of the landscape from the top are breathtaking. In addition, there is a wonderful bonus for those that make the effort of the climb – a cool and crystal clear natural rock pool perfect for bathing.

This tranquil and peaceful place is indeed a hidden gem in Matale. Visitors who come to the areas in Matale for holidays are strongly recommended to visit this beautiful place.

How To Reach Bambarakiri Ella
Bambarakiri Falls are located approximately 200 m away from a bend of the Matale-Pallegala-Hasalaka mountain road and almost 5 Kms uphill from Rattota town. From the Matale city, the distance is nearly 16 Kms.

One can also book private cabs to Matale or Kandy and finally towards the Rattota town and then a nature trail towards the fall.

Public transport towards Matale and Kandy is also available.

The Wall Painting Museum of the Central Cultural Fund is located in Dambulla. It displays paintings that were discovered during the Dambulla Project's surveys and research since 1991. The museum's theme is 'Evolution of Sri Lankan Cave and Wall Paintings.' It also aims to protect paintings that have been damaged by natural causes and insects.

The Dambulla Museum is unique in Sri Lanka since it is the only one dedicated to exhibiting Sri Lankan paintings, including the famous Sigiriya frescoes and wall paintings at various ancient temples from the Anuradhapura period to the modern era. The museum opened on November 16th, 2003.

I would like to extend my heartfelt gratitude to Mrs. Pushpa for her assistance during our visit and for helping us discover valuable books at the bookstore.
review_3: The Museum of wall Painting of the Central Cultural Fund is located in the Rangiri Dambulla Raja Maha Vihara premises in Dambulla.This purpose of this museum is to allow witness and study the wall paintings that were uncovered by the Dambulla Project through surveys and researches carried out since 1991.
review_4: IF you're at the Dambulla Royal Rock Temple you might as well stroll over to the Dambulla-Museum of Wall Paintings to check it out. It's a series of periods of Sri Lankan art from cave paintings up to the 20th century. It's just a sampling. It's RS 300 slash 2 WHOLE dollars. Right outside & towards Kandy is a HUGE Bodhi tree to gaze at and offer your blessings to the future (the kids) of Sri Lanka.
review_5: A little treasure trove of education regarding art styles from prehistoric to pre-modern times in Sri Lanka, it is a must visit for art connoisseurs. Ms. Pushpa who manned the desk was extremely helpful. There is also a little bookshop with rare books. The museum is situated on the main Kandy-Jaffna road, right next to the golden temple. Parking is available.

Wahakotte Church is a beautiful and historically significant place of worship with deep-rooted Catholic heritage in Sri Lanka. The church’s architecture is impressive, with a serene and peaceful environment that makes it an ideal place for prayer and reflection.

The surrounding area is picturesque, and the church is well-maintained, preserving its historical charm. The devotion of the local community is evident, making the experience even more meaningful. Whether you are visiting for religious purposes or to explore Sri Lanka’s rich history, Wahakotte Church is a must-visit.

Highly recommend stopping by if you’re in the area!

The St. Anthony’s Church or St. Anthony’s Shrine is the cathedral church of the Roman Catholic Diocese of Kandy in Wahakotte, Sri Lanka. The church is located in Wahakotte, in the north of Matale District, about 32 km (20 miles) from Matale and is dedicated to Saint Anthony. The church is one of the most sacred pilgrim places for Catholics in Sri Lanka and a national shrine. The Benedictine mission is attached to the church.

Considered to be one of the most important pilgrim sites for catholics in the country, people from all over Sri Lanka come to visit it at least a couple of times every year. It has also been given the title of a national shrine. The light coloured building is crowned with brick red tiled roof and the interiors are beautifully decorated with intricate elements. Enjoy a trip to this church and soak in its blissful atmosphere.

Wahakotte was known as “Wasala-Kotte” – Castle in the Fort. It is believed that there was a palace and a fort, where the present church is situated, the village was so called, because it protected the palace, traces of which are still visible. There is another legend to say that “Waha”-poison; “Kotte” – pillow; means poisonous pillow because a queen had committed suicide after hearing of her husband’s death at war.

History records that when king Dutugemunu defeated king Elara, in 164 B.C. he had built a small fortress at Wahakotte. There are still some ruins of ancient buildings on a rocky-hill called “Maligatenna” – Palace-field.

These ruins show that Wahakotte was once a prosperous town with a fort, ramparts, a moat and thorough fares, where sub-kings lived. One can see Dambulla Rock Temple to the north from Maligatenna. The Ceylon Alamanac records that there were ruins of “Rangalle Nuwara” and a Hindu Kovil – “Pattini Devalaya” in the woods close by.

The history records that the first christian Monarch – Yamasinghe, adopted son of Karalliyadda Bandara, who later became Don Phillip after embracing christianity in Goa, India, was received by some rebels from Kandy and was crowned at Wahakotte by the Portuguese.

It is also recorded that Don Francisco Wijekoon, who organised the Kandyan rebels against king Rajasinghe, too, was present on this occasion.

King Rajasinghe of Kandy, who fought against the Portuguese captured some of them and made them settle down in Wahakotte. These captured people earned their living by engaging themselves in cultivation, business and trade, and women sewing dresses for women, men sewing dresses for men; some engage themselves in running taverns and making liquor.

The people of Wahakotte are slightly fairer in complexion, compared to other rural people in the area, though there are also very dark people among them. Because of this complexion neighbouring villagers still refer to them as “Pruthugeesi” or the decendants of “Parangi”. Some had Kandyan names mixed with Portuguese names, like Don Juan Wijeratne or Dona Issabella Ekanayake.

When the Dutch captured the areas that were under the Portuguese rule, they confiscated all catholic institutions, churches, schools and religious places, chased away the Catholic priests and banned all religious activities.

review_1:
Nagolla Rajamaha Viharaya, located in the Kurunegala District of Sri Lanka, is a significant Buddhist temple with a rich historical background. It is traditionally believed to have been constructed during the reign of King Vatta Gamini Abaya, also known as King Walagamba, between 103 BC and 89 BC. This period corresponds to a time when the king sought refuge from South Indian invaders, highlighting the temple's historical importance as a place of sanctuary and devotion.
•  Historical Background: The temple's association with King Walagamba underscores its long-standing significance in Sri Lankan history. It is thought to reflect the king's efforts to promote Buddhism during a tumultuous period.
•  Architecture: The temple showcases traditional Sri Lankan Buddhist architectural styles, with beautifully carved stonework and various statues of the Buddha, which are central to Buddhist worship.
•  Location and Accessibility:
•	The temple can be easily reached from the Alauwa Bus Route in Kurunegala.
•	After traveling approximately 2.5 km on this route, you will encounter Dharmapala Mawatha.
•	A right turn into Dharmapala Mawatha leads you another 1.5 km to the temple.
•  Community and Activities: Nagolla Rajamaha Viharaya serves as a hub for local Buddhist practices, hosting religious ceremonies, meditation sessions, and educational programs for both the community and visitors.

Galle Fort area is unlike any other place we’ve found in Sri Lanka; an atmospheric town where culture, religion, and different architectural eras collide in the tropics. Popular with tourists, it’s still very much a living town both within and outside the fort walls (and we definitely do encourage you to explore the wider Galle town beyond the Fort!).

I ❤️ 😍 💖 ❣️ 💕 💘 ❤️ 😍 💖 ❣️ this historical site. Is one of the most preserved structures in history. It took me a few hours to explore..
review_3:
Visiting Galle Fort at night was an unforgettable experience. The moment I stepped inside the old stone walls, I felt a sense of calm and wonder. The entire fort had a peaceful atmosphere, very different from the daytime buzz. The narrow cobbled streets were softly lit with warm streetlights, and the cool breeze from the ocean made the walk even more refreshing.

The historic charm of the Dutch architecture stood out beautifully under the night sky. Many small shops and cafés were still open, offering a cozy and welcoming vibe. Some had soft music playing, and the aroma of coffee and local food filled the air. The view from the fort walls was breathtaking — looking out at the dark sea under a sky full of stars was truly magical.

There were couples walking hand-in-hand, friends laughing, and even solo travelers like me simply enjoying the moment. Everything felt safe and relaxed. Galle Fort at night is perfect if you’re looking for a quiet place to reflect, take photos, or just enjoy the beauty of history meeting the ocean.

It’s not just a place to visit, it’s a place to feel. I highly recommend experiencing it after sunset — it has a charm you won’t find anywhere else.
review_4: One of the beautiful mix of history and coastal vibes. Narrow streets old buildings cafes a different thing. Walking on fort walls with the sea on one side is the best part during sunset. Must visit place
review_5:
Walking through the arched entrance of the Galle Dutch Fort felt like stepping back in time. The cobbled streets were lined with charming colonial-era villas, their red-tiled roofs glowing under the tropical sun. I spent the afternoon wandering along the massive granite ramparts, watching the turquoise waves crash against the ancient walls below.
​The iconic white lighthouse stood guard against the deep blue sky, a perfect picture of resilience and beauty. As evening fell, the narrow lanes buzzed with life—cafés serving iced tea and boutiques selling local crafts. It was a magical, timeless blend of Sri Lankan charm and European heritage.

5/5 – Iconic Historical Church in Galle Fort

The Dutch Reformed Church in Galle is one of the oldest and most iconic landmarks inside the Galle Fort. You can simply walk inside and look around freely, and it really feels like stepping back in time.

The architecture is beautiful and still preserves the colonial Dutch style. One of the unique things you’ll notice is that the floor is lined with old tombstones, which makes it both historic and a little unusual compared to other churches.

It doesn’t take long to explore – around 10 to 15 minutes is enough to walk through, admire the details, and take a few photos. Still, it’s absolutely worth visiting as part of your Galle Fort tour because it highlights the history and culture of this coastal city.

If you’re in Galle, don’t miss this stop – it’s quiet, peaceful, and full of stories from the past.

Japanese Peace Pagoda – Rumassala is one of the most peaceful places we visited near Unawatuna. The atmosphere is calm, quiet, and perfect to relax your mind. The pagoda itself is beautiful, clean, and gives an amazing spiritual feeling.

The Japanese Peace Pagoda at Rumassala is a truly serene place. Set on a hill above Unawatuna, it offers breathtaking panoramic views of the ocean, Galle Bay, and even the Galle Fort in the distance. Sunset here is especially beautiful. Recommended to visit in the morning or evening, since afternoon is too hot.

The stupa itself is spotless white and surrounded by thick jungle, creating a peaceful and spiritual atmosphere. It was built by Japanese monks, and you can often hear soft chanting or ringing of the temple bell, which adds to the calmness.

Access is easy by tuk-tuk, or you can hike up through the Jungle Beach trail if you prefer a bit of adventure. Entry is free, though donations are welcome. Free vehicle parking is also available. Just remember to dress modestly as it’s an active place of worship.

We visited the Dutch Hospital Precinct with my wife and our 10-year-old child, and it was one of the most charming spots in Colombo! The colonial architecture is beautifully preserved, and the evening atmosphere was lively yet relaxing.

description: Longstanding tower featuring a large clock face, built in 1883 on top of a former guard room.
display_review:
Standing tall at the entrance of the historic Galle Fort, the Galle Clock Tower is one of those landmarks that immediately grabs your attention and stays in your memory long after you leave. On my visit, I was struck not just by its imposing structure but also by the history it carries within its walls. Built in the 19th century during the British colonial era, the tower feels like a timeless guardian, overlooking the fort and the vibrant city beyond.
What makes the experience special is the setting. Surrounded by the ramparts of Galle Fort and lush greenery, the clock tower offers a stunning vantage point. From here, you can look out over the Indian Ocean, with waves crashing against the rocks below—a breathtaking contrast of stone and sea. It’s the perfect spot for photography, whether you want to capture the tower itself or the panoramic views stretching in every direction.
I enjoyed strolling around the area, soaking in the atmosphere of the fort, and then pausing by the tower to simply admire how seamlessly history and nature blend here. It’s also a great meeting point, as many locals and visitors alike gather around the tower before exploring the rest of the fort.
What adds to the charm is the sense of calm despite being in a popular destination. Standing under the tower, you can hear the ocean breeze and the distant chatter of people enjoying their day. It’s peaceful, yet alive with stories.
For anyone visiting Galle, the Clock Tower is more than just a quick photo stop—it’s a chance to feel connected to the heritage of the city. A simple yet powerful symbol of Galle’s colonial past, it’s definitely worth the visit.

review_5:
Galle Fort is a fortified old city built originally by the Portuguese in the late 16th century, and then extensively fortified by the Dutch in the 17th and 18th centuries. It’s a UNESCO World Heritage Site, both because of its colonial heritage and its relatively well-preserved city structure, architecture, and ramparts.
The thick stone walls/ramparts are one of the most iconic features. You can walk around them and enjoy ocean views, the sea breeze, and sunset.
At the southeastern tip of the Fort on Point Utrecht Bastion. Good for views, photos, and enjoying the ocean panorama.
A heritage building from the Dutch colonial period. Now converted into a dining & shopping precinct. Great place to have meals, relax, and see colonial architecture blended with modern uses.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Galle, Sri Lanka.
display_review: Only built in 1904, Meeran Mosque exhibits a fabulous mix of architectural styles that again showcases the amazing diversity of cultures that have lived in and ruled over Galle throughout the years. While its Baroque facade looks more like that of a cathedral, some incredible Islamic detailing and decorations remind us that we are indeed looking at a mosque. Add in various British Victorian features, Dutch-influenced designs, and patterned Italian floor tiles, and you start to get a snapshot of not only the history and architecture of the mosque but that of Galle as well. The elegant whitewashed building certainly stands out against its surroundings. It is this unusual and unique combination that makes Meeran Mosque so worth visiting.

description: Simple home of a Sri Lankan author with displays on Buddhist culture, set in tree-dotted grounds.
display_review:
A must-visit for anyone interested in the authentic heart of Sri Lankan culture. This museum is a true treasure trove of rural heritage, beautifully showcasing a vast collection of traditional tools, crafts, and household items that feel like a step back in time. It's also a surprisingly great spot for families—the kids in our group were completely fascinated by the old bullock carts and horse-drawn carriages on display.

Beyond the fascinating artifacts, the true magic is in the setting. The museum is surrounded by lush, serene gardens and the natural beauty of Koggala, making the entire experience incredibly calm and peaceful. It’s the perfect place to wander, reflect, and enjoy the tranquility. All in all, it’s a wonderful, insightful, and highly recommended experience for visitors of all ages looking to connect with the island's past amidst beautiful nature

This is a really good place to visit if you want to understand Sri Lankan culture and history.

We had a great time looking at all the fascinating folk exhibits and learning about traditional life.

The museum is well-maintained and very informative. It's a wonderful glimpse into the past. Highly recommended for anyone in the area!
review_3:
This place is not only showcasing the birthplace of the famous Author Mr Martin Wickramasinghe but it also had used the premises to preserve the unknown and unseen heritage of Sri Lanka.
The new generation can get an idea about the ways and means of how things happened centuries ago in Sri Lanka. The traditional methods of fishing and other industries, how the people dressed, the vehicles used back then they are all exhibited in this place. The various trees are named too. It is a calming place with such natural beauty.
There is also a book shop where the books of Mr Martin Wickramasinghe could be bought for a discounted price. The ticket for local adult is 100 Rs and for children it is only 50 Rs per child.
It is highly recommended to visit this place if you wish to learn more about our heritage and traditions.
review_4: My favorite museum in Sri Lanka, the objects are in good shape and the museum is situated inside a botanical garden. Very relaxing. Friendly staff. Do use mosquito repellent, especially in the are near Martin Wickramasinghe birth house.
review_5:
Take a step off the main road in Koggala and into the cooler and shadier confines of the Martin Wickramasinghe Folk Museum. This lovely site offers a restful leap back into the history of Sri Lanka.

After buying your ticket, you will be free to roam the tranquil gardens that surround the museum. The highlight of the museum is the simple, unassuming house where the famous author Martin Wickramasinghe was born. Simply stepping inside the building feels intimate and special; you will have the opportunity to view his antique writing desk, one of his books, and other objects that inform you of the type of person Mr. Wickramasinghe was at a glance.

The museum itself is a plethora of ancient and interesting items from village life, and everyday living. You will witness a large bullock cart, naturally, a traditional fishing boat, and various tools that have long been used for agricultural purposes. You will find yourself in hallways filled with colorful folk masks, puppets, and several neutral-toned utensils used to cook in kitchens from years ago.

As you stroll quietly through the gardens of the museum from one exhibit to the next, you will appreciate the "stepping back in time" aspect of the museum. The museum comprehensively reflects and preserves Sri Lankan culture and a simple way of life for all to witness and appreciate.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Galle, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
Kushtaraja Gala

This more than life size relief carved in solid rock is thought to be one of the best statues in the Mahayanist Buddhism in Sri Lanka. The origination of this sculpture is not known but many theories and folklore exists on the creation of the statue. It is also believed that this statue belongs to the period of 7-9th centuries.

Kushtaraja Gala statue at Weligama can be found on the old main road, which goes through the Weligama town and is about 12 Km away from the Matara town. To reach the site coming form Galle you should take the old road before you reach Ahangama across the railway track. This is turnoff is located adjoining the Weligama Bay Rest House. You will see the sculpture on your right just before the second railway crossing. This statue is part of the Kushtarajagala Viharaya.
review_1:
Kushta Raja gala is located in Weligama in Matara District.
Kushta Raja gala famous for its Rock carvings stature of Awaloketiswara bodhi sathwa in Mahayana budhism.

This statue date back to the in between 6th to 7th Century AD. Central feature of the statue around 9 foot tall and it shows ability of Sri Lankan artistory. This statue is a prominent representation of the Mahayana Buddhist believed.

There're several legends and filklore are related with this statue.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Galle, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
Sinigama Devol (Sinigama Devol Devalaya) is a famous shrine located near Galle, just north of Hikkaduwa.
It is dedicated to Devol Deviyo, a deity believed to protect people from evil influences, malicious individuals, and misfortune.

Special features:

The shrine is located on the coast, with the original shrine situated on a small island in the sea.

The original shrine can only be reached by boat.

It has a long history of religious and folk beliefs.

Devol Maduwa is a well-known ritual performance held here:

Mostly takes place during May–June.

Involves traditional dances, songs, and religious rituals to drive away evil spirits and seek blessings.

Ceremonies often continue throughout the night, blending religious devotion with music and dance.

review_4:
Seenigama Devalaya, located near Hikkaduwa on a small island facing the southern sea of Sri Lanka, is a unique and spiritually significant shrine dedicated to Devol Deviyo, a local deity revered for his protective qualities, especially among fishermen. This site draws numerous pilgrims and visitors, offering them a blend of cultural heritage and scenic beauty.

The shrine is divided into two parts: the main temple on the mainland and the island temple, known as Seenigama Muhudu Viharaya. The island temple is accessible only by boat, which adds an element of adventure and exclusivity to the pilgrimage experience. Visitors can only reach it during designated prayer times, reinforcing its sacred atmosphere.

Devol Deviyo is known as a protector of fishermen and their boats, embodying a deity that not only safeguards livelihoods but also addresses grievances. Many visitors come to seek justice or retribution against wrongdoers; this practice involves grinding black pepper (ගම්මිරිස් අඹරනවා ) at the shrine while invoking the deity's power to impose punishment on adversaries.

The temple complex features modern architecture that harmonizes with its spiritual purpose. Ample parking facilities enhance accessibility for visitors.

The experience at Seenigama Devalaya is enriched by its stunning natural surroundings. Visitors can enjoy breathtaking sunsets over the ocean. The shrine also hosts a vibrant perahera season, attracting crowds eager to witness traditional processions and festivities.

While many come for pilgrimage purposes, others are drawn by folklore surrounding Devol Deviyo, which adds layers of intrigue to the visit.
review_5:
Seenigama is a small village on the southwest coast on the main Colombo-Matara roadway. Located just before Hikkaduwa. The Seenigama Devalaya  (shrine) is on a small island a short way from the beach.

The Seenigama Devalaya dedicated to the Devol Deviyo, or deity Devol, is a popular destination for people coming from all parts of the country to make offerings at the shrine. Devol is a local god, who protects the fisherman and their boats and from more recently buses and lorries.

description: Laid-back hotel in a Dutch Colonial building offering sea views, plus an airy restaurant & a bar.
display_review:
Review: Recent Stay at Closenberg Hotel

Staff:
The staff are extremely kind, warm, and genuinely helpful. Their friendliness truly stands out. With that said, some additional training—especially in telephone etiquette and professional communication—would greatly enhance the overall guest experience.

Room & Bed:
The rooms have a charming vintage character, and the bed is super comfortable, ensuring a great night’s sleep. However, housekeeping needs closer attention, particularly proper dusting and overall cleanliness. While a dental kit is not provided, it’s not a major concern as this seems to align with the hotel’s concept.

Bathroom:
The bathroom is an area that requires improvement. The bidet feels outdated; a bidet shower would be more practical. A reliable hot water system with good water pressure is essential and should be addressed.

View & Balcony:
The view from the balcony is absolutely unbeatable—one of the best I’ve experienced. For better privacy, it would be great if balconies could be partially screened, as neighboring balconies are visible.

Pool:
The infinity-style pool (the type that visually merges with the ocean) is located in a truly unique, one-of-a-kind spot with breathtaking views. This area has huge untapped potential. A pool bar, ambient lighting, bean bags, music, Wi-Fi, and drinks could transform it into a major Instagrammable attraction and a strong revenue generator for the hotel.

Food:
The food is amazing and absolutely delicious—clearly one of the hotel’s strongest points.

Atmosphere & Maintenance:
Despite its charm and potential, basic maintenance has been seriously neglected. Issues noticed include lack of garden grooming, food placed on the floor in hallways for cats, a generally run-down appearance, broken gutters, and water leaking during rain—even inside the hotel, with containers placed to collect water. A broken stairway leading to the ocean is overgrown with weeds. These are fundamental issues that need urgent attention.

Restaurant & Coral Displays:
Large coral installations inside the restaurant take up significant space and appear old, poorly maintained, and neglected, with dried plants and unkept fish tanks. Similar coral displays were seen in other areas. It would be far better if these were relocated back to the ocean, allowing the restaurant space to breathe and function as intended.

Landscaping:
The path to the pool is surrounded by greenery, but much of it feels unplanned. The hotel would benefit greatly from a proper horticultural redesign.

Overall:
This hotel is ideal for honeymooners due to its limited number of rooms and romantic setting, but more work is needed before it can fully cater to a niche market. Personally, I love this hotel—it offers one of the best views I’ve ever experienced. It’s truly sad to see such an iconic property slowly losing its charm due to neglect rather than lack of potential.

I sincerely hope management takes serious note of these points and works toward restoring Closenberg Hotel to its former glory.

review_1:
Ambalama refers to traditional wayside rest shelters in Sri Lanka, often found near ancient roads, pilgrimage paths, reservoirs, or under shady trees. These structures were simple, open-sided, and supported by wooden or stone columns, providing a place for travelers to rest, reflect, and engage in communal activities.

History and Significance
- The origin of Ambalama dates back over a thousand years, with some of the earliest examples emerging during the Anuradhapura and Polonnaruwa periods.
- Ambalama served not only as physical shelters but also as spiritual and social gathering points, hosting bana sermons, public meetings, and dispute resolutions.
- These structures represented a timeless tradition of generosity, hospitality, and spiritual mindfulness embedded in Sinhala-Buddhist civilization ¹.

Architecture and Design
- Ambalama were built using wood, stone, or clay, with designs varying according to location and purpose.
- Some notable examples feature intricate wood carvings, such as the Panavitiya Ambalama, which showcases elaborate carvings comparable to those at Embekka Devalaya and Lankathilaka Vihara.
- The structures often included a pinthaliya, a large pitcher made of clay or stone, for fetching clean water ² ³.

Notable Examples
- *Panavitiya Ambalama*: Famous for its ornate wood carvings, this Ambalama is a masterpiece of traditional Kandyan craftsmanship.
- *Kadugannawa Ambalama*: Built in the early 18th century, this Ambalama provided shelter for merchants visiting the Kandyan kingdom.
- *Mangalagama Ambalama*: According to folklore, this Ambalama was built during the reign of King Dutugemunu.
- *Giruwa Ambalama*: Constructed by Queen Sunethra Devi for devotees of the Dedimunda devalaya in Aluthnuwara, Kegalle ³ ².

Conservation Efforts
- The Department of Archaeology and local heritage organizations are working to document and restore remaining Ambalama structures, preserving not only the architecture but also the cultural ethic of shared humanity and mutual aid ¹.
