review_2:
A Hidden Gem of Ancient Art and Adventure

Kurullangala is truly a hidden gem that left me in awe on both of my visits. The ancient rock art here is simply mesmerizing, offering a rare glimpse into the lives and beliefs of those who lived thousands of years ago. These exquisite drawings, believed to date back to the prehistoric era, primarily depict various birds, hence the name “Kurullangala” (Bird Rock). The detailed representations of these birds, along with other symbols, are thought to have been created by early humans, possibly as part of their spiritual or ritual practices.

The hike up to the site is a bit challenging, but the experience is well worth the effort, especially when you reach the top and take in the breathtaking views. The secluded nature of the site, combined with its historical significance, makes it a perfect spot for those interested in both adventure and history.

On my second visit, I decided to camp overnight on the rock. While the experience was a bit eerie and made for a slightly restless night, it added a thrilling element to the adventure. The isolation and the ancient surroundings truly make you feel connected to the past in a unique way.

If you're up for a mix of history, art, and a touch of adrenaline, Kurullangala is a must-visit. Just be prepared for a bit of a hike and an unforgettable experience!
review_3:
Kurullangala in Ella is a rich and beautiful environment and attractions there include prehistoric paintings of different kinds of enormous birds. I thoroughly enjoyed hiking in this beautiful nature area which provided an enjoyable experience full of adventure and excitement.

There are prehistoric sculptures and drawings and they are spread over a large area extending to higher altitude on rocks, The drawings are spread in a large area and some were sketched on a yellowish rocky area. Small streams flow through the area of the hike and diverse flora, and fauna are also common. The scents and sounds of nature are all around and the trail takes you through these beautiful areas and showcases spectacular views.

Sleeping on a rough surface, walking about 20 kilometers to the destination, carrying a backpack through the thick jungle, and baking under the blazing sun in open areas was made easy by Decathlon equipment I carried.

Our guide was "Meththananda" inform him in advance helpful guide.
review_4: Kurullangala possesses a set of cave arts which are believed to be a few thousand years old. A proper research has not yet being carried out by the relevant authorities to find the origin of these valuable cave arts. The climb up here is a strenuous one and is not for the light-hearted. You must go with necessary equipment (at least a rope) and an experienced guide, unless you are a pro rock climber.
review_5:
"Kurulangala" with mysterious bird image 🕊🦅
Kurullangala 😮😮
Unsolved mysteries are still hidden in this small Sri Lanka. Some mysteries are still far from people even today.
Perhaps due to the difficulty of going to such places, there is less awareness about these places.
This is the information about such a mysterious and wonderful place.
Khundargala is a stone with ancient paintings located in Uva province.
Uva province itself is an area that hides a lot of mysteries of the Lankan dynasty.
There are two main factors that have influenced this rock to get the name of Kringargala
Among them, one of the things is that even Sri Lankans can see rare species of birds in this area.
A very small species of bird is also said to emit a sharp sound from their mouth.
In this way, one of the reasons for calling it the name of the birds is the painting on the rock. These paintings are located about 100 meters below the top of the mountain range.
Even today, such paintings are not seen anywhere in Sri Lanka
Among these paintings, there are peacock paintings, peacock feathers, palm prints, stripe patterns, and faded paintings that cannot be clearly identified.
Among them is an image of a reptile with a pointy back and a tail whose head has been erased.
The unique feature among the paintings on this rock is the delicate technique used to decorate the paintings.
Among them, it is unique that the figures are decorated using stripes and circles.
Similarly, the colors that have been used have been protected from the sun for centuries.
A special kind of paint has been used for this purpose. Similarly, there is no direct resemblance to any old painting style in the world in the bird paintings.
About 100 paintings that have been left in various decays can be seen in Khurdarigala today.
These paintings may have been drawn by a group of people who settled in this country a long time ago.
But the surprising fact is that there are paintings on this rock at a place where it is very difficult to climb to see the paintings and about 20 feet above.
No support for drawing these paintings can be seen around it.
Today, tourists are attracted to see the paintings. It is an extremely difficult journey.
It is very dangerous to go on this journey without proper preparation and without proper understanding. The entire mountain is full of dry plants.
At first sight it looks like a very small distance but you have to cover a long distance to go on this journey. It is really a very tiring journey.
You have to use rope supports to pass some places. Even so, it is not easy to pass such places because the slope is very dangerous.
This is why this journey requires both physical strength and mental strength.
It is also a great privilege to be able to describe the hidden facts of Sri Lankan history in this way. And look at these beautiful places and be careful not to destroy that beauty..

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Monaragala, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
The ruins of the ancient Rambakan Oya giant rock canal lie close to the new Rambakan Oya Reservoir project in Ampara. The canal has been built with massive interlocking granite slabs which kept the rocks in position even in the worst conditions. A nearby slab inscription (known as Rambakan Oya Slab Inscription) which has been identified as belonging to the 12th – 13th centuries identifies that the granite canal was built by Liyana Nayakayan and Kanathkan Vahanse living in the Gal Weta area.

Rambakan Oya is a tributary of Mundeni Aru, which flows through the Borders of Moneragala and Ampara. The Rambaken Oya has been dammed and the surrounding area was irrigated in the ancient past by king Saddhatissa (137-119 BC) and king Maha Parakramabahu (1153-1186). But only the ruins of this amazing canal remain today from ancient times.

This twin canal system built with massive granite slabs each interlocking with each other has been restored for about 100 meters above the ground level. Traveling another 800 meters long this road will bring you to the newly completed Rambakan Oya Reservoir completed in July 2013
review_1: Dematamal Viharaya. One of hidden gem in Sri Lanka . Surrounded by lush greenery, offers calm and quite space where the mind can truly relax. Bla beautiful ancient temple
review_2:
The Dematamal Viharaya is a historic and significant Buddhist temple located in the Okkampitiya village of the Monaragala District in the Uva Province of Sri Lanka.
Key points include:
• Ancient Origin: It is considered one of the oldest stupas in Sri Lanka, believed to date back as far as the 3rd or 2nd century BC, during the Anuradhapura Kingdom period.
• Legendary Significance: According to local folklore, this temple served as the hiding place for Prince Saddhatissa after he was defeated by his elder brother, Prince Dutugamunu, in a battle for the throne at a nearby location called Yudaganawa. The intervention of the monks at the Viharaya is credited with bringing about the eventual reconciliation of the two brothers.
• Archaeology: The complex features a typical ancient brick stupa, a Bodhigara (shrine for the Bodhi tree), an image house, and ruins of meditation cells, reflecting the architectural style of the Anuradhapura era.
• Location: The temple offers a serene setting, often situated amidst picturesque paddy fields near the town of Buttala.
review_3:
The Dematamal Viharaya can be seen in the Monaragala District, situated in the middle of a beautiful paddy field about six kilometers along the Buttala –Okkampitiya road, which branches off a short distance after traveling along the Buttala–Sellakataragama road from the Buttala town.

A special feature here is the guard stone carved with both a female and a male figure together. The Department of Archaeology states that such a guard stone cannot be found at any other location in Sri Lanka.

Following the arrival of Buddhism with Mahinda Thera, the Buddhist revival that took place in Anuradhapura also influenced the Ruhuna region. According to the Mahavamsa, King Kavantissa of Ruhuna constructed several temples such as the Tissa Maha Vihara, Situlpawwa, Kutaliya, and Gamtha Valiya, and consistently offered alms to the Sangha in all four directions. It is mentioned in the Pujavaliya that King Kavantissa also built a temple named Dematamal.

Uva Wellassa, home to vast archaeological sites such as Maligavila, Dam̆begoda, Yudaganawa, and Buduruwagala, was once a prosperous region with a rich economy and vibrant culture. Historical chronicles mention that the Dematamal Viharaya, located in this region, is associated with several significant events. One such account states that Prince Tissa, after being defeated in battle by Prince Dutugemunu, sought refuge beneath the bed of the chief monk of this very temple.

Best place to visit and teach some historicaly important lessons to your kids if you visit Kataragama since only one hour journey from there. But be careful when you visit Kataragama - Buttala road there are several wild elephants are on the road.
review_4:
This is one of the famous ancient temples in Sri Lanka which is in Buttala - Okkampitiya road. The temple is in a close proximity to the main road and surrounded by green paddy fields. The scenic view from the temple is amazing.

review_5: Dematamal Viharaya is an ancient Buddhist temple located in Okkampitiya, near Buttala in Sri Lanka. According to legend, Prince Saddhatissa sought refuge in this temple after being defeated by Dutugemunu. The temple features a well-preserved stupa and other ancient structures, reflecting its historical and religious significance. It is a serene site, surrounded by lush greenery, making it an important cultural and spiritual landmark in Sri Lanka.

description: An ancient irrigation reservoir in Monaragala, built by kings to support local agriculture.
display_review:
Handapanagala Junction sits along the A-2 road from Wellawaya toward Tissamaharama—nearly 9km from Wellawaya bus stand. From the junction, a left turn leads to the Handapanagala reservoir, nestled in the Uva Province's Monaragala District.
Handapanagala Junction is more than just a roadside turn; it's a portal to tranquility, heritage, and wildlife. Whether you’re passing through or planning to pause, here’s what makes it worth your time:
*Serene Ambiance: Scenic reservoir set against rural, rocky backdrops, ideal for reflection or photography.
*Wildlife Encounters: Possibly visit with elephants—especially during sunset.
*Historical Depth: Ties to ancient kings and nearby archaeological temples add cultural depth.
*Accessibility: Convenient location along A-2, within a short drive of Wellawaya or Buttala

review_2:
Neelagiriseya (Sinhalaː නීලගිරිසෑය) is an ancient colossal Stupa situated in Lahugala, Ampara District, Sri Lanka. It is the largest Buddhist Stupa in the Eastern Province of the country. It has a circumference of 182 m (597 ft) and 22 m (72 ft) height in the current status. In the recent history the Stupa and its monastery site had been neglected and abandoned over three decades as the rise of activities of military organization LTTE (Tamil Tigers) in the area.

Neelagiriseya is believed to be built by either King Kavan Tissa (205–161 BC) or King Bhathikabaya (20-9 BC) and has been called as Uttara Seevali Pabbata Viharaya in ancient times. According to an inscription belongs to the 1st century, found during the archaeological excavations done in 2011, describes about a grant to the temple by the Maharaajinee Chula Sivalee Queen, a daughter of the King Bhatikabaya. In historical resources Bhatikabaya is described as a viceroy, who reigned in Ruhuna when King Kutakanna Tissa (42-20 BC) was ruling the country. Another inscription recovered from the site in 2011 excavation, also reveals about donations made by king Jettatissa I (263-273) or by the King Jettatissa II (328-337 CE).

The first reference about the Nilgiriseya in modern history could be found in the early decades of the 20th century. A.M. Horcart, who had visited this place in 1928, has published some details about the Stupa after 2 years later of his journey.
review_3: Ancient temple in Monaragala which is under construction now.
review_4:
Neelagiri Seya  (Nilagiri Seya) is the largest Buddhist stupa in the Eastern Province. The stupa, located in the Lahugala Forest Reserve has been neglected for over 1200 years. It has been inaccessible 30 years since 1980 due to the LTTE Terrorist activities in the area. The stupa is believed to have been built by King Kawantissa (3rd century BCE) or King Bhatikabaya (20 BCE-9 CE) and has been renovated in the 7th century.

These days the stupa is being built. Anyone willing to help with construction is welcome.

review_1: The history of this pond runs back to the Polonnaruwa Period. In the 11th and 12th centuries, the southern part of Rohana had been divided into two major territorial divisions along the Walave Ganga River (Nicholas, 1963)
review_2:
This place is located about 100 meters south of the main road, about 10 miles from Monaragala, on the Monaragala-Ampara road (263 km), near a mountain.

The special pond, called “Galabedda Biso Pokuna,” is believed to have been built in the 12th century. According to stories, it was used by Sugala Devi, a queen in Sri Lanka during that time.

The pond is made of stone and was part of a larger structure that included toilets and other facilities. The stones from the walls, toilets, and pavilions still show the foundation of the original building.

Water flows into the pond through four stone dragon spouts placed in each direction. There are also four entrances to the pond, carved beautifully in stone, one in each direction.

description: A 7,360-foot high mountain peak with a footprint-shape feature & pilgrimage site accessed by trails.
display_review:
We went to worship Sripada in the season.

It took 3 hours to reach the top as we followed the shortest route (Rathnapura). It was a little tired but an enjoyable hike.

There were many many shops from the beginning to the top of the mountain. People can eat and rest at those places.
Prices of foods also increase with the altitude.

Some points there were stairs in steep angles but are manageable.

At dawn, temperature was around 8° celsius. Rest room was not cold because of the body heat of the crowd.

On the top there were police officers to control the crowd. At that day we also received breakfast freely by the Police. To climb down it took only 2 1/2 hours as we came straight without resting.

Nice experience ❤️

review_2:
Climbing Adam’s Peak during the daytime in the off-season is a completely different experience — quiet, peaceful, and beautifully raw. Unlike the busy season with crowds and lights, the mountain feels calm and untouched, almost like you have the whole trail to yourself.

The daytime sunlight brings out the true beauty of the surroundings. On the way up, I could clearly see the valleys, waterfalls, tea estates, and endless greenery. The silence of the mountain, mixed with the sound of birds and the cool breeze, made the climb feel refreshing and meditative.

The steps are still challenging, especially the steep sections towards the top, but the slower pace and open views make the journey enjoyable. There are fewer shops open during off-season, but that adds to the natural, authentic feel of the climb.

Reaching the summit in the daytime is a special experience — no rush, no crowd, just pure peace. The temple at the top is very calm, and the panoramic view spreads across the mountains like a giant painting. Even without the sunrise, the beauty of the landscape is breathtaking.

Climbing down in the daylight is much easier and gives you even more time to enjoy the scenery. The weather can change quickly, so clouds might roll in suddenly, but that only adds to the magical atmosphere of the mountain.

Overall, a daytime off-season climb at Adam’s Peak is perfect for anyone who enjoys nature at its purest — quiet trails, real adventure, and no crowd. It’s a peaceful, refreshing, and deeply memorable experience
review_3:
Climbing Sri Pada is truly one of the most remarkable and rewarding experiences in Sri Lanka. We went during the off-season, and it was a completely different kind of adventure — peaceful and quiet, with very few people on the trail. Most of the shops along the way were closed, except for a few, especially one near the Maha Giri Dambe area, which was a welcome stop for a short break.

The climb is around 3–4 km one way, with approximately 4,500 steps from the bottom to the summit. Sanitary facilities are available along the trail, though many may not be functional during the off-season. Before starting the climb, we had to inform both the Nallathanniya Police and the Forest Conservation Department at the Makara Thorana area to obtain permission. There was also a fee of over Rs. 1,000 payable to the Forest Department to access the Siripada walkway during the off-season.

Despite the challenges, the journey was truly mind-blowing — the cool air, misty surroundings, and sacred atmosphere make it an unforgettable spiritual and physical experience. Whether you witness the iconic sunrise from the top or not, the sense of accomplishment and peace after reaching the summit is beyond words.

A must-do experience for travelers from all around the world!
review_4:
Adam’s Peak (Sri Pada) is a truly unforgettable experience.
The climb is challenging but incredibly rewarding, especially reaching the summit at sunrise. Watching the sky change colors while surrounded by misty mountains is magical. The spiritual atmosphere, with pilgrims from different faiths walking together, makes the journey even more meaningful. The path is well-marked with resting spots along the way, and the views throughout the climb are breathtaking. A must-visit place in Sri Lanka for nature lovers, adventure seekers, and anyone looking for a peaceful, soul-touching experience
review_5:
Sri Pada is a mountain in central Sri Lanka, about 2,243 meters (around 7,360 ft) high.
It is considered sacred by multiple religions. For Buddhists, there is a footprint-shaped rock near the summit believed to be that of the Buddha. Hindus say it’s of Shiva, some Christians and Muslims believe it’s the footprint of Adam or other figures.
Many pilgrims climb overnight so they can reach the summit in time to see the sunrise (“Ira Sevaya”).
Along the journey there are customary stops: places to rest, small shrines, places for offerings.
The trails go through beautiful landscapes: tropical forests, mist-covered hills, tea plantations, streams.
The surrounding area is part of the Peak Wilderness Sanctuary, which has rich biodiversity and is a protected natural area.
Watching the sunrise from the top is one of the most memorable parts. The view, the light, the feeling of being above the clouds — many say it’s unforgettable.
Also, at sunrise, due to the shape of the mountain, a triangular shadow is cast onto surrounding terrain — a visual phenomenon many visitors try to witness.

The atmosphere is incredibly calm — even with many visitors, the entire area feels blessed and spiritually grounding. Walking around the temple grounds, you can really sense its historical value and the cultural heritage of Sabaragamuwa.

A must-visit place if you want to experience true Sri Lankan tradition, history, and serenity. A very special and meaningful location. 🙏✨

review_2:
Ratnapura Pothgul Viharaya is a remarkable historical and spiritual site that offers a unique glimpse into Sri Lanka’s Buddhist heritage. According to tradition, the temple dates back to the reign of King Valagamba (89–77 B.C.), who faced a period of exile when South Indian invaders seized the throne. During his escape to Sabaragamuwa, King Valagamba stopped at a home in Wadumulla, which belonged to the Ewaduwo of the Goviwansa family. An elderly woman noticed the tired king but did not recognize him. King Valagamba asked for some water, which she kindly provided. While he was drinking, she offered him a meal, saying, “I see you are suffering from hunger; you may eat something.” She served him a plate of rice with several curries. The king began eating each curry separately, one by one. Observing this, the woman advised, “Do not eat it like the Mahakalu Deyyos’ war strategy” (Makakalu Deyyo is a nickname for Valagamba). “Mix all the curries with the rice, and you will enjoy the full flavor.

The king was puzzled and asked, “What does my way of eating have to do with the Mahakalu Deyyos fighting?” The woman explained, “If you wish to win the war, you must release all your powers together, combining all your forces, just as you combine all the curries with the rice. Only then can you succeed by using all your capabilities in harmony.” King Valagamba realized the wisdom in her words and marked the rock near her home, which later became known as Hunugala. After regaining the throne, he summoned the woman and honored her by granting her the village, which came to be called Wadumulla Ewadu Nindama.
The Ewaduwo played a key role in constructing Ratnapura Pothgul Vihara, preparing war equipment, cultivating paddy, and advising the king in his capacity as Purohitha. Later, the village was formally recognized as Purohithagama. The Ewaduwo also learned Tamil at Demalaporuwa, contributing further to administration and cultural knowledge.

Perched on a rock and accessible via a climb of approximately 454 steps, Ratnapura Pothgul Viharaya offers a serene and secluded atmosphere. Inside, visitors can see a large reclining Buddha statue carved from living rock, flanked by deities such as Saman and Vishnu near the statue’s head. The site also features natural elements like the nearby “Daru Pokuna” pond, believed by locals to have medicinal properties. While its remote location, limited visitor facilities, and minimal signage may pose challenges, the temple’s historical significance, spiritual ambiance, and scenic surroundings make it a truly memorable destination. Ratnapura Pothgul Viharaya is ideal for heritage enthusiasts, spiritual travelers, and anyone interested in exploring Sri Lanka’s rich Buddhist history.

review_3: The Delgamuwa Raja Maha Viharaya, located in Kuruwita in the Ratnapura district of Sri Lanka, is considered a historically important Buddhist holy site in the country. The reason for this is that the Sacred Tooth Relic was safely hidden in this temple for 43 years. This temple is located about 1 kilometer from Kuruwita on the Colombo-Ratnapura road.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        There is a great historical story behind this. King Buwanekabahu V brought the Tooth Relic from Kurunegala to the Kingdom of Kotte (1375-1391). However, with the Portuguese invasion in 1505, concerns arose regarding the safety of the Tooth Relic.                                                The three sons of King Vijayabahu VII divided the Kingdom of Kotte, the King Buwanekabahu VII ruling the Kotte Kingdom, King Mayadunne of Sitawaka and King Raigambandara of Korala in Paswalallawita.                                           King Buwanekabahu VII had no successor and his daughter's son, Prince Dharmapala, ruled the kingdom. But he was a puppet of the Portuguese.  Therefore, the custody of the Tooth Relic was given to King Mayadunne, the ruler of Sitawaka.                                       Accordingly, the Tooth Relic, which was in Kotte, was brought to the Delgamuwa Viharaya.                                                              Later, during the reign of King Wimaladharmasuriya I, the Tooth Relic was brought to the Kingdom of Kandy.
review_4:
An ancient temple that has now been renovated.

This temple is where the tooth relic of Buddha was hidden during a Portuguese invasion.

During the 13th Century the tooth relic was placed in Kurunegala, it was then moved into Kotte. Later in the 15th Century during the Portuguese invasion the tooth relic was at a risk and the kings and the Buddhist monks at that time hid the tooth relic in a "Kurahan Gala"(Grinding stone) until the threat from the Portuguese went away. The Kurahan Gala is on display at the temple.

The temple also has 4 recently constructed Devalaya.
review_5:
Delgamuwa Rajamaha Viharaya, located in Kuruwita, is one of Sri Lanka’s most historically significant temples, especially for those interested in Buddhist heritage. This sacred temple is best known as the hiding place of the Sacred Tooth Relic of the Buddha during the 16th century, when it was concealed from Portuguese invaders inside a grinding stone.

The temple itself is peaceful and beautifully maintained, surrounded by lush greenery and offering a quiet retreat from busy city life. The main shrine room and stupa are simple yet spiritually powerful, reflecting the deep devotion of its history. Visitors will find informative plaques and guidance from resident monks about the temple’s past, making the visit both educational and spiritually enriching.

The surroundings are calm, with the gentle sounds of nature adding to the meditative atmosphere. There’s a sense of stepping back in time as you walk through the grounds and imagine the events that once took place here.

Delgamuwa Rajamaha Viharaya is not only a religious site but also a cultural treasure of Sri Lanka. Whether you are a pilgrim, a history lover, or a traveler looking to experience authentic heritage, this temple is a must-visit location in the Ratnapura District.

description: An ancient and sacred Buddhist temple in Ratnapura, reflecting Sri Lanka's deep religious heritage.
display_review:
Ganegama Aramunapola Rajamaha Viharaya, which is also commonly referred to as Ganegama Rankoth Viharaya, lies 2.5 kilometers before Pelmadulla Town in the center of a paddy field. The history of the temple goes as far back as King Devanampiyatissa (250-210 BC) of the Anuradhapura Kingdom.

According to the historical chronicle Bodhi Wansa, this temple was one of the locations where one of the first 32 samplings of the Sri Maha Bodhi was planted by King Devanampiyatissa. This Bodhi tree can be seen even today at the temple.

In the early days, this was called Denawaka Viharaya. The Chronicles again speaks of this temple during the reign of King Parakramabahu I (1153-1186) of the Polonnaruwa Kingdom. It is said that King Vijayabahu I established an army camp at a basin (ද්‍රෝණය) between Kiribathgala Kanda and Kuttapitiya hills which is believed to be this location. Thus this temple also has been called Dronawakka Viharaya (Denavaka Viharaya) in one era. This is the name that has been used to identify this temple in the Vihara Asna (Nam Potha), which is believed to have been published during the reign of King Parakramabahu IV (1411-1463) of the Kotte Kingdom as the reading book for students.

The Buddhist order faced a major decline with no proper priesthood during the reign of King Vijayabahu I (1070-1110), and he invited higher-ordinate priests from Burma (now Myanmar) to ordain local priests and reinstate the priesthood in Sri Lanka. This temple has been frequently used by priests who arrived from the Arammana area of Burma. The Arammana had become Aramunupola with time and is now known as the Aramunapola Rajamaha Viharaya.

King Parakramabahu IV (1411-1463) of the Kotte Kingdom, who had a deep bond with his mother, developed many temples in honor of his mother, in addition to the building of one of the greatest spiritual and historic Pirivenas of that time. One such temple to receive his attention was Ganegama Aramunapola Rajamaha Viharaya, where he had built a shrine using the best stonemasons from upcountry and low country and assigned the temple to the Pepiliyna Sunethra Devi Pirivena.

Therefore it is considered that this temple is one of the few temples in which both up-country and low-country styles of stone masonry can be seen in a single place. The building is built using 27 exquisitely carved rock pillars.

At two ends of the roof of this building, you can see two golden pinnacles, and due to these, the temple also became known as Ganegama Rankoth Viharaya.

Portuguese captain Dom Jerónimo de Azevedo, during his stint in then Ceylon between 1594 and 1612, is said to have used Denavaka as a camping ground on one of his attempts to attack the Kandyan Kingdom. When news arrived that the Portuguese army was marching towards Denavaka looting and destroying all Buddhist temples lying on their path, the villagers had managed to hide a large Buddha statue carved out of wood and other sacred items by submerging them in the Denaka River. Most of the valuables of the temple have been looted by the Portuguese.

This 4-foot statue was later recovered and lies in the main statue house even today. There are some other Buddha statues and animal statues in the image house belonging to the Kandyan era. All these have been made by filling clay onto a wooden frame.

Italian art expert Luciano Maranzi in 1968 attempted to restore the faded paintings in this temple during his visit to restore the famous Sigiriya Frescos, which were vandalized by unknown persons. During this attempt, he managed to recover a painting with a Portuguese soldier and 2 cats within these paintings, consolidating the stockade of Azevedo at this temple.

Bopath Falls in Ratnapura is a truly beautiful waterfall, surrounded by lush greenery and a serene natural setting. The waterfall’s unique Bo‑leaf shape adds a special charm, making it one of the most picturesque spots in Sri Lanka. The entrance cost is very low, making it accessible for anyone looking for a simple yet memorable nature experience.

From the entrance, there is a short and easy walk to the main viewing area. The waterfall looks absolutely stunning from this safe zone, with the sound of rushing water adding to the calm and refreshing atmosphere. It’s a great place to take photographs, enjoy a peaceful moment, or spend time with friends and family.

However, it is extremely dangerous to climb the waterfall. According to the guards at the site, around 88 deaths had been reported up to the date of our visit, mostly caused by people attempting to climb or get too close to the slippery rocks. For safety reasons, it is strongly recommended to enjoy the waterfall only from designated safe areas.

Parking is available nearby, with a small charge for the larger lot about 200–300 meters from the entrance. There is also a smaller parking space closer to the gate for convenience.

Overall, Bopath Falls is a must-visit attraction in Sri Lanka. It offers stunning natural beauty at a very low cost and is perfect for families, friends, or solo travelers. While safety is essential, the experience is truly unforgettable and highly recommended for anyone visiting Ratnapura.

review_2:
Bopath Ella is a 30-metre waterfall in the Ratnapura District, Sri Lanka, named for its resemblance to the leaf of the sacred Bo tree.

There is a designated car park operated by the local council (Kuruwita Pradeshiya Sabha). Vehicles must be parked there, and from the parking area you walk to the viewpoint.

A small ticket fee is required to access the waterfall area (Locals Rs. 50 and Tourists Rs. 300).

A toilet complex is constructed near the waterfall by the local council. There are also private restrooms (some attached to places with lodging).

Along the footpath from the parking/viewpoint to the waterfall there are small shops/vendors offering snacks, drinks, and local food. Also there are guest-houses/restaurants nearby with more substantial meals.

There is a viewing area (platform or stairs) near the falls. You have to walk from the car park through a shaded, scenic path. Rocks may be slippery; caution advised.

The historical significance of the devalaya, coupled with its peaceful surroundings, creates an atmosphere of reverence and tranquility. The site's protection by the archaeological department ensures the preservation of its cultural legacy for generations to come.

review_2:
Bolthumbe Saman Devalaya is about 1Km away from Balangoda-Bogawanthalawa road at Balangoda in  Rathnapura district in Sri Lanka.
According to the legend, the history of this holy place dates back to the period of the king Ravana. This devalaya premises is considered to be the place where the final battle between the king Ravana and Rama took place and in the end, the king Ravana defeated Rama, so it is believed that this place is historically important.
The present devalaya was built by the king Seethwaka Rajasinghe I in the 15th century with the intention of protecting the valuables in Rathnapura Maha Saman Devalaya from the Portuguese invaders: therefore, the layout of Bolthumbe Devalaya is similar to that of Rathnapura Maha Saman Devalaya.
A prominent place is given to King Ravana in all its activities. During the annual procession which is held in August, the Ravana Flag is carried in the front of the procession. People gather from far and wide to visit and pay homage to it. Further, devotees regularly get together to perform the rituals.
This is really a very calm place because it is free from the hustle and  bustle of the town. The cool atmosphere refreshes the tired visitors. The situation is far more favourable in the early ours in the day as we can enjoy the fresh mountain air. The spectacular mountain landscapes, far-stretched wooded mountain slopes and the mountain tops kissing the slow moving masses of white clouds further create a pleasant picture in one’s mind. As a result, one really feels mental relaxation.
It is worth visiting this holy place in your life time.

Once the residence of Ehelepola Nilame, a regional officer under King Sri Vikrama Rajasinghe, this site now beautifully preserves the essence of Sri Lanka's past. The exhibits range from prehistoric artifacts to more recent historical items, including a fascinating display of ancient gem mining techniques for which Ratnapura is world-renowned. The museum also features replicas of endemic animals and a lush, well-maintained garden that adds to the experience. As a history enthusiast, I found the blend of regional stories and national significance deeply enriching. A must-visit if you're exploring Sri Lanka’s interior.

display_review:
Budugala Archaeological Site, nestled in the quiet village of Kalthota in the Ratnapura District, is a hidden gem for history lovers and nature seekers alike. This ancient forest monastery offers a peaceful, untouched atmosphere and is rich with ruins dating back to over 2,000 years.

Visitors will find stone inscriptions in early Brahmi script, the remains of ancient stupas, moonstones, meditation chambers (Padhanagharas), and a unique narrow stone bridge—all surrounded by lush greenery. The upper rock surface even features mysterious petroglyphs that add to the mystique of the site.

Though not as commercialized or maintained as major heritage sites, its raw, untouched nature adds to the charm. Ideal for quiet exploration, reflection, and photography, Budugala is perfect for those looking to connect with Sri Lanka’s ancient past in a serene setting.

This Stupa, known as Kotavehera, is also known as Sutighara Chaityam.  Kotavehera can be reached after going about 3 kilometers on the Galapitamada road from the Nelundeniya junction on the Colombo Kandy road.  On the Avissawella Kegalle road, you can turn from Pitgaldeniya junction and come here via Tuntota.

Ketanagama is a historic town in Sri Lanka.  Evidence has been found in the area that confirms that the area was inhabited at least during the Mesolithic period.  In the past, this village was known as 'Punkhagama'.  Also, villages named Jathagama, Dathigama, Pilagama etc. are mentioned in old records as well as genealogies.  'Punkha' in Pali means back or sheath of arrow.  It is also known as Datta in Sinhalese and 'Punkkhagama' or Dathigama was changed to Katanagama by Kata Vahara who traveled far.
----------------------///////----------------
This same stupa, also known as Dedigama Kotavehera, is also known as Sutighara Chaitya. Kotavehera can be reached by traveling about 3 km from Nelundeniya Junction on the Colombo-Kandy Road on the Galapitamada Road. It can also be reached by turning off from Pitagaldeniya Junction on the Avissawella-Kegalla Road and via Thungthota.

Dedigama is a historical town in Sri Lanka. Evidence has been found in the area to confirm that this area was inhabited at least in the Middle Stone Age. In ancient times, this village was known as ‘Punkhagama’. Similarly, in ancient documents and chronicles, this village called Dedigama that we find today is known by the names Jatagama, Dathigama, Pilagama etc. In Pali, ‘Punkha’ means the back or the part called the quill of an arrow. It is also called ‘Datta’ in Sinhala and ‘Punkhagama’ or ‘Dathigama’ has been transformed into ‘Dedigama’ through a long-distance oral tradition.

description: Sacred, historic temple said to be visited by the Buddha with paintings by a renowned artist.
display_review:
A Sacred Treasure of Sri Lankan Buddhism
The Kelaniya Raja Maha Vihara is one of Sri Lanka's most ancient and revered Buddhist temples, constantly filled with devoted worshippers and curious visitors from around the world. This sacred site is not just a place of worship—it's a living masterpiece of art and history.
What makes this temple truly extraordinary are the stunning murals and hand-painted artworks that adorn its walls. These paintings showcase the unique style of Sri Lankan Buddhist art, created by master artists whose work has drawn admirers from across the globe. The intricate detail, vibrant colors, and spiritual storytelling in these murals are unlike anything you'll find elsewhere. Art enthusiasts and historians travel from far and wide specifically to witness these remarkable creations.
The temple grounds are always bustling with activity, but there's something magical about visiting in the evening. As the sun sets, the temple comes alive with a serene beauty—the soft lighting, the sound of evening prayers, and the peaceful atmosphere create an unforgettable experience.
What makes Kelaniya Raja Maha Vihara especially sacred is that it houses precious relics of Lord Buddha, enshrined within the temple's pagoda (dagoba). According to tradition, the Buddha himself visited this site during his third visit to Sri Lanka, making it one of the most significant pilgrimage sites for Buddhists.

Practical Tips for Visitors:
Don't forget to buy flowers to offer to the Buddha—there are plenty of flower vendors available near the parking area. The temple has ample parking space, but it's important to note: do not leave your valuables in the vehicle. It's highly recommended to carry your belongings with you or have someone stay with the vehicle for security.
Whether you're a devotee seeking spiritual solace or a traveler interested in culture and history, this temple offers a profound and beautiful experience. Highly recommended for anyone visiting Sri Lanka!
review_1:
However, according to legend, this temple was built by King Gotabhaya who ruled the Ruhunu kingdom in the 2nd century BC. However, according to Bell’s Kegalle Report, this temple was built by King Gotabhaya (253-266) of the Anuradhapura kingdom. But according to the palm leaf manuscript, “Wattaram Asna” (වට්ටාර‍ම් අස්න) this temple complex has been built by the deity named Viskam (Vishwa Karma) on the request of Maha Brahma who had divided the land to 12 sections and built 12 aramas at the site.

Unfortunately, this temple has been modernized with no regard to the antiquary.  You will find remains of two buildings belonging to the Anuradhapura era today.

Ruins of the ancient Mahamali Paya (මහාමලී පාය) which is said to have been built in the model of Lohamahapaya in Anuradhapura. Instead of 1600 pillars, this has been built using 35 granite pillars. The pillars are 10 feet 6 inches high and 1×1 feet in size. Five rows each 0ccuping seven pillars had held the five storied building.  According to legend, this is a five-story building with the upper floors built of wood.

Close to this ruins of a smaller building  which has been the residence built by king Gotabhaya for Maliyadeva Thero. Escavations carrieds out at this building in 2017 has unearthed rare bronze figures of possibly a guard and a two elephant statue at about a deapth of about 1 meter.

එයට බමුණු වඩනා නමකුත්         වේය

In 2012, the Department of Archaeology excavated a mound of earth in a coconut grove belonging to the temple and unearthed a medium-sized stupa. This stupa has been built on an octagonal platform with a brick retaining wall. The stupa and the platform have been completely restored now.  However, the relics of the stupa had been stolen by treasure hunters long before its recent discovery.  According to the display board erected by the archaeology department, this stupa indicates features of the Anuradhapura kingdom era and based on the legend, this possibly would have been built by King Gotabhaya (253-266).

The hexagonal image house at the temple belongs to the Kandyan Era. The entrance to this image house is through an exquisitely carved large wooden door frame and a door. The statues and the paintings in the image house still retain their originality even though the paintings are now in a dilapidated state due to lack of maintenance.

Another image house from the same era is found with a mandapa in front. In front of this building lies an ancient Siri Pathul gala (foot imprint of Buddha) and an ancient pillar inscription. This inscription describes the donation of twenty kalanda of gold to the Sirisangabo Rajamha Vehera for the provision of meals to the priests on the full moon poya day of the month of Medin (March) in the 7th year of King Abaya Salamevan (914-923). It also mentions that if any monk gives food prepared with these funds to any other outside the monastery, he will be expelled from the viharaya.

The temple is also one of the four recipients of the pure gold (damba-ran) Buddha statues which were carried from India by Maliyadeva Thero. These four statues were kept at Wattarama Rajamaha Viharaya, Kothmale Puhulpitiya Rajamaha Viharaya, Hanguranketha Madanwala Rajamaha Viharaya and Padiyapelella Morapaya Rajamaha Viharaya. This statue was stolen some time ago and had been recovered sans some parts from the arms which had been cut away by the plunderers.

You can reach this temple from Polgahawela (5km) through Moragolla and from Kegalle (8km) through Devalagama.
review_2:
Wattarama Sri Arahanta Maliyadeva Raja Maha Viharaya is situated in the Kurunegala District of Sri Lanka. It is an ancient Buddhist temple with a rich history dating back many centuries. The temple holds great significance for Buddhists, both locally and nationally, as a place of worship and pilgrimage. The temple is dedicated to Arahanta Maliyadeva, a revered figure in Buddhism. The temple's architecture typically reflects traditional Sri Lankan Buddhist temple design, featuring intricate carvings, colorful paintings, and statues of Lord Buddha and other deities.

Devotees visit the temple to engage in religious practices such as meditation, offering prayers, and participating in religious ceremonies.
The temple often hosts cultural events and religious festivals, attracting visitors from far and wide. Wattarama Sri Arahanta Maliyadeva Raja Maha Viharaya may be located amidst serene natural surroundings, offering a tranquil atmosphere for spiritual reflection. Besides its religious functions, the temple also serves as a community hub, providing support and services to the local population. Efforts are made to preserve the historical and cultural heritage of the temple, ensuring that future generations can continue to benefit from its teachings and spiritual ambiance.
review_3:
Wattarama Sri Arahantha Maliyadeva Rajamaha Viharaya is an temple with a history of over 2300 years lying between Polgahawela and Kegalle. This temple is so named due to its close association with Maliyadeva thero who is believed to be last Maha Arahath of Sri Lanka.

According to the Manoratha Purni Atuwa in Anguththara Nikaya (මනෝරථ පූර්ණී අංගුත්තරනිකාය අටුවාව), Maliyadeva Thero has been last Arhath in the ancient Sri Lanka although some dispute this claim.
According to legend, this temple has been built by king Gotabhaya who ruled the Ruhunu kingdom in the 2nd century BC. However according to Bell’s Kegalle Report this temple has been built by king Gotabhaya (253-266) of Anuradhapura kingdom. But according to palm leaf manuscript, “Wattaram Asna” (වට්ටාර‍ම් අස්න) this temple complex has been built by the deity named Viskam (Vishwa Karma) on the request of Maha Brhama who had divided the land to 12 sections and built 12 aramas at the site.

Unfortunately this temple has been modernized with no regard to the antiquary.  You will find remains of two buildings belonging to the Anuradhapura era today.

I went to see this on Vesak day and there was a huge crowd. Very noisy and not a place to relax. The collection of donations and the noise of the shops inside the temple were annoying. It was completely a business place.
review_4:

According to legend, this temple has been built by king Gotabhaya who ruled the Ruhunu kingdom in the 2nd century BC. However according to Bell’s Kegalle Report this temple has been built by king Gotabhaya (253-266) of Anuradhapura kingdom. But according to palm leaf manuscript, “Wattaram Asna” (වට්ටාර‍ම් අස්න) this temple complex has been built by the deity named Viskam (Vishwa Karma) on the request of Maha Brhama who had divided the land to 12 sections and built 12 aramas at the site.
review_5: Wattarama Sri Arahantha Maliyadeva Rajamaha Viharaya has a special connection with Maliyadeva Thero, the last Arhath in ancient Sri Lanka according to historical records. His profound spiritual quest led him to enter the priesthood and later find his way to the Maliyadeva Rajamaha Viharaya. Here, at a remarkably young age, he achieved Arhathhood, after delving into the profound teachings of Buddhism. The temple holds an air of mystery with its conflicting historical accounts. Some sources claim it was built by King Gotabhaya in the 2nd century BC, while others believe it was constructed by the deity Viskam (Vishwa Karma) on the request of Maha Brahma. Despite modernization, Wattarama Sri Arahantha Maliyadeva Rajamaha Viharaya still bears ancient remnants. Notably, you can explore the ruins of the Mahamali Paya, said to be modeled after the Lohamahapaya in Anuradhapura, showcasing 35 granite pillars and possibly five stories. Nearby, you'll find the remains of a smaller building that once served as King Gotabhaya's residence for Maliyadeva Thero.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Kegalle, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
Located along the Avissawella – Ginigathhena road, Barandi Kovila is a historical site accessible just past the Sitawaka Oya bridge. Marked by an Archaeological Department signboard on the right side of the road from Avissawella, the site welcomes visitors through a gate.

Upon entering, three distinct terraces unfold, with the upper terrace revealing the mesmerizing ruins of Barandi Kovila—a square structure adorned with stone pillars. The stone railing surrounding these pillars showcases intricate carvings, featuring elegant motifs of leaf and floral designs, as well as captivating bahirawa faces. The delicately carved water spout adds a unique touch, while guard stones with carved full pots stand at one entrance.

Notably, hidden within the stone carvings, a surreptitious elephant in one corner of the terrace sparks curiosity. The first terrace surprises with an unexpected carving of a parrot, distinct and seemingly out of place—an artistic diversion that adds an element of intrigue to the site's historical richness

review_2:
The Barandiya Kovil, is the only stone monument that remains from Sri Lanka’s 16th century Sitavaka kingdom. It’s disputed whether the Hindu temple is from the Sitavaka period or earlier and if it was ever completed.

You have to walk around 300m to reach this place from the road. It is having a great historical value. The 4 rating is for the place is small and nothing major to check on the site.
review_3: Historic Kovil from 1500AD. People who looks after the site are polite, explains well about the site and history. Was not crowded at the time of the visit. Free small car park available. Road to the site is narrow. Free entry.
review_4: Fine archeological monument which is located just 60 km from Colombo. According to the Archeological department this place is built by King Mayadunne (1521AD-1581AD). Located very close to Seethawaka river and easily accessible via Awissawella- Hatton main road.
review_5: This is a historycal place near by Awissawella town just 1 km from the town towards Kegalla. Ancient monument of the Seethawaka Rajasinghe King's Era. Very calm and quite place to visitors who are interested about. There are many carved stone has been used for build this Hindu Kovil. The place is protected by archeological department.

Disappointing Experience – Incorrect Order & Unjust Charges

I ordered one chicken fried rice and one chicken noodle. However, the waitstaff incorrectly added other dishes to my bill. When I pointed this out and cancelled the extra items, they still charged me for them.

To make matters worse, they claimed that the prices had been revised, even though the menu hadn’t been updated to reflect the changes. As a result, I ended up paying an additional LKR 1600 on top of my original bill.

This kind of service is unacceptable. I strongly advise others to avoid this restaurant due to their poor handling of orders and lack of transparency in pricing.

description: Built in the 16th century, this preserved Dutch sea fort houses a network of museums, shops & cafes.
A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Kegalle, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
Galabedda Biso Pokuna (Queen’s Pond) is a fascinating archaeological site located very close to the main road near Monaragala. Parking is easy on the roadside, and it’s just a short 50m walk to reach the site, making it very convenient to visit.

This pond is believed to have been part of the regional palace complex of Princess Sugala Devi, a remarkable figure from the 12th century Polonnaruwa Era. The site includes remains of moats, ramparts, and palace structures, giving a glimpse into the grandeur of that time.

The pond itself is an architectural marvel, carved out of solid rock about 10 feet deep, with four beautifully designed inlets where water once flowed through the mouths of mythical ‘Makara’. Another interesting feature here is the carved toilet stone, showcasing the advanced sanitary systems used by the elite centuries ago.

I visited in September 2025 during the dry season, so the pond had no water, but it was still impressive. The site is well maintained and does not take more than 15 minutes to explore, making it a perfect quick stop to experience history.

A must-visit if you’re traveling in the Monaragala area — it offers a unique blend of history, architecture, and culture.

review_2: Good place
review_3: A natural pool with a waterfall

review_1:
This steel bridge over the Gurugoda Oya lies in the Niloluwa village in Mabopitiya in Kegalle District. The bridge has been built in the Government Factory by a British engineer named E. C. Davies in 1897. This engineer has been instrumental in building number of bridges in Sri Lanka during this time.
The bridge was short cut to remote villages such as Udabogala, Udovita, Kammellawatta, Nelligoda and Hikgoda. However due to poor quality of the repair, the bridge was again in a unusable condition by 2012. This 125 year old bridge was declared a protected archaeological monument and in around 2014, it was repaired again by the Department of Archaeology. Currently this is in a good condition.
Traveling through Avissawella, you need to travel for 44km though Ruwanwella, Bulathkohupitiya and Alawathura to the site. Travelling through Kegalle, you need to travel for 13km on the Bulathkohupitiya road, passing Hettimulla and Horonthota. Either way, take the Mabopitiya Degalaeriya road (B599) and travel 500 meters to reach the Archaeology board indicating the bridge location.
review_2:
125 year old ancient steel bridge constructed in 1897 when Sri Lanka was under British rule.
Structure designed by English engineer C. A. Davies and manufactured at the Kolonnawa Government factory.
Bridge lies over a picturesque natural pool fed by the surrounding hillside water resources.

This is situated in Kegalle district Sabaragamuwa province and is a protected archeology monument.

Lies in a highly scenic tea and rubber plantation hill area. 💚

review_1:
When you travel along Kandy road passing Kadugannawa area,  look across the valley to witness a peculiar shaped rock at the distance. The shape reminds you of the famous rock fortress Sigiriya but it is in fact “Bathalegala” or “Bible Rock” as the British named it.

About the rock

The height of the rock is stated as 797.7 m in the survey map. The breadth spans a whopping 710 m across the rock surface. The surface is mostly flat covered in mana and some woods here and there.

If you are starting from the trailhead, the elevation gain would be around 150m. You can also start the hike from Hathgampola town. In that case, the elevation gain would be around 500m.

How to get there?

Bathalegala is located close to Aranayake. Whether you’re coming from Colombo or Kandy, turn onto Aranayake road at Mawanella. Follow the road until Gevilipitiya, turn right at the junction and drive about 2km to Hathgampola town. From there you’ll have to turn right to a narrow road (Bathalegala road) with a signboard “to the Bathalegala road” printed in Sinhala.

Following this road, you’ll reach a junction where you should turn left to a narrower road which runs past the trailhead.  After driving 2.5 km on this road, look towards your right to notice the starting point. There is a signpost so it is hard to miss. Parking space is available but limited.

Route

Mawanella ➡ Ussapitiya ➡ Gevilipitiya ➡ Hathgampola ➡ (Bathalegala road) ➡ (Turn left at the first junction) ➡ Trailhead (14 km)
review_2: Spectacular view. Relatively easy climb. Concrete steps for most part. Narrow yet maintained road you can ride in a vehicle half way.

Whether you're walking along a serene lakeside trail or trekking through a vibrant national park, hiking offers stunning views and a chance to connect with the outdoors. It’s not just about the destination—it's the experience of being immersed in nature, breathing fresh air, and challenging your body. Plus, hiking is suitable for all skill levels, with trails available for beginners to seasoned adventurers. A rewarding, peaceful, and healthful activity.
review_4:
Wonderful experience!

Hike takes around half an hour at medium pace, for one way. The surroundings are safe.
It would be advisable to hike in the mornings or evenings.
Spectacular scenery at the summit!
review_5:
Summary :
It's an easy hike that takes about 30 minutes (depending on the person) to climb from the trailhead. It takes approximately a 3 hour drive with moderate traffic from Colombo. Don't climb Bathalegala if there is even a small shower cause stepping rocks can be extremely slippery. There are two clearings for camping on the top.

Details:
Bathalegala aka Bible rock is a mountain located in Aranayaka, Kegalle. This has an elevation of 700m above sea level. The top surface is mostly flat and spans several hundred meters.
It took a three hour drive with moderate traffic for me to reach the trailhead (I live in Borella). The route I took was Kandy rd to Kegalle then Kegalle-Karawanella rd then Gewilipitiya-Hettimulla rd then I took a concrete road that is a left turn from Hathgampola town.
Just setting Bathalegala as your destination in google maps is alright. This concrete road is a narrow rough approx. 3km road so you need a vehicle with a moderate ground clearance and a smaller wheel base might be handy. Fyi I drove this road with no passengers in a toyota aqua but I scratched my underfloor panels few times. There's paid parking (abt 100 rs) at the end of this concrete road.
The hike takes about 30 minutes and I would say it's easier than climbing Sigiriya. There are no water sources in the peak so taking a water bottle is necessary. The trail is pretty clear so there's no need to worry about getting lost. There are no leeches in this area even in the rainy seasons.
At one end (South) of the peak there's a small stupa and on the North end you can see Kabaragala, Sri pada, Asupini ella, Ambuluwawa, Rakshaca, Samasara and Urakanda mountains if there's no mist. Views can be pretty good at the sunrise and the sunset so don't miss!

The historic Mawanella Brick Bridge is a charming landmark that can be beautifully seen from the banks of Ma Oya. Surrounded by greenery, it’s a calm and quiet place, making it ideal for families who want to spend some peaceful time together. The bridge itself is a reminder of colonial-era engineering, still standing strong with its unique brick design.

If you’re passing through Mawanella, this is a nice spot to stop, relax, and take a few photos of the bridge and the serene surroundings.

✅ Good for: families, history lovers, photographers, nature breaks

review_3:
The Mawanella Old Brick Bridge is a historical landmark located in Mawanella, Sri Lanka, along the Colombo-Kandy route. Constructed between 1832 and 1833 during the British colonial period, this bridge is a testament to early engineering marvels. It spans approximately 70 meters and is supported by four arches, all built using specially crafted bricks. Remarkably, the bridge has never undergone major renovations, yet it remains operational for vehicular traffic, showcasing its durability and craftsmanship.

The bridge holds cultural significance, as it was constructed under the supervision of Captain Brown during the tenure of Governor John Fester. It also has a spiritual connection, with a shrine built nearby to honor local beliefs and ensure the smooth completion of its construction. The Mawanella Old Brick Bridge is not only a functional structure but also a piece of history that reflects the ingenuity of British engineering and the cultural heritage of the region. It is a must-visit for history enthusiasts and those exploring Sri Lanka's colonial past.
review_4:
As someone who has grown up in Mawanella, the Brick Bridge has always been a landmark close to my heart. This historic bridge was built in 1833 during the British colonial period and is one of the oldest surviving brick bridges in Sri Lanka. It was constructed entirely with bricks and lime mortar, without using cement, showcasing the brilliant engineering techniques of that time.

The bridge was an essential part of the old Colombo-Kandy road, connecting many towns and supporting the tea and spice trade routes for decades. Its beautiful arches and solid brickwork have stood strong against floods and heavy traffic over nearly two centuries. Walking across it as a child, I always admired the reddish-brown bricks, the elegant design, and the peaceful Maha Oya flowing beneath.

Today, although a new concrete bridge runs parallel for modern traffic, the brick bridge remains a proud symbol of Mawanella’s heritage. Sadly, it is often neglected, with littering around the area and no proper preservation measures. I truly hope local authorities and the community work together to protect this priceless historical structure for future generations.

If you visit Mawanella, take a moment to stop by this timeless bridge, feel the breeze, and admire the engineering brilliance that still stands strong since 1833.
review_5:
The Mawanella Old Brick Bridge is a notable landmark in Mawanella, Sri Lanka, known for its historical and architectural significance. Here are some details about the bridge:

1. **Location**: The bridge is located in the town of Mawanella, which is in the Kegalle District of the Sabaragamuwa Province. It spans the Maha Oya River, an important waterway in the region.

2. **Historical Significance**: The Old Brick Bridge is one of the oldest brick bridges in Sri Lanka. It was constructed during the British colonial era and is a testament to the engineering skills of that period. The exact date of construction is not well-documented, but it is believed to have been built in the late 19th or early 20th century.

3. **Architecture**: The bridge is constructed primarily of bricks and features traditional colonial architectural elements. Its design includes multiple arches, which provide both structural support and aesthetic appeal. The use of bricks and the arched design are characteristic of the engineering practices of the colonial period.

4. **Cultural Importance**: The bridge is an important cultural and historical landmark for the people of Mawanella and the surrounding areas. It stands as a symbol of the town's heritage and colonial past. Over the years, it has become a point of pride for the local community.

5. **Current Status**: While the Old Brick Bridge is no longer the primary crossing over the Maha Oya River due to the construction of newer bridges, it still holds historical value. It may not be used for heavy vehicular traffic but often serves pedestrians and light vehicles.

6. **Tourism and Preservation**: The bridge attracts visitors interested in historical structures and colonial architecture. Efforts have been made to preserve its structure and maintain its historical integrity. It is a popular spot for photography and for those interested in the history of the region.

7. **Surrounding Area**: The area around the bridge is picturesque, with the river and lush greenery providing a scenic backdrop. It is a pleasant place for a stroll and offers a glimpse into the town's historical landscape.

The Mawanella Old Brick Bridge remains a cherished historical monument, reflecting the rich colonial heritage and architectural history of the area.

display_review:
I was truly amazed by how it blends history, spirituality, and adventure all in one place. It’s more than just a hike — it’s a journey through time, and what makes it unforgettable is the breathtaking view of Sigiriya Rock from the top.

🏯 History and Culture
Pidurangala was once an ancient Buddhist monastery dating back to the 1st or 2nd century BC. It became especially important in the 5th century AD when King Kashyapa moved monks here after turning Sigiriya into his royal fortress. Along the way, I came across ancient caves, a stupa, and a massive reclining Buddha statue, all of which added a deep spiritual feel to the climb.

🥾 The Hike Itself
The hike took me around 30 minutes — the first half was steps, and the second half involved a bit of bouldering and scrambling to reach the top. It was slightly challenging but definitely fun and rewarding. Since the trail starts inside an active temple, I had to remove my shoes and dress modestly, covering my shoulders and knees.

I went early for sunrise, and it turned out to be the highlight of my trip. Watching the golden light touch Sigiriya Rock from Pidurangala’s summit, surrounded by misty forests, was simply magical — a view that stays with you long after you leave.

💰 Why I Recommend It
For just 500–1000 LKR ($3–$6), it’s a great value — quieter, cheaper, and less touristy than Sigiriya. Many people say it offers the best view in Sri Lanka, and after seeing it myself, I completely agree.

If you have the time, I highly recommend doing both Sigiriya and Pidurangala — one for its rich history and royal ruins, and the other for its stunning natural views and adventurous spirit. Together, they perfectly capture the beauty and depth of Sri Lanka.

On our way to Colombo, we specifically chose to stop at Ambepussa Rest House because we expected clean washrooms — which, unfortunately, turned out to be a major letdown. The facilities were smelly and poorly maintained. When we informed the staff, one member simply sprayed air freshener, which did nothing to address the actual issue. We had to ask again about 20 minutes later for a proper cleaning.

As for the food, we were hoping to order à la carte since most of us aren't heavy rice eaters. However, both the receptionist and one of the waitstaff discouraged us from doing so, saying the kitchen was busy with the buffet. We ended up ordering just an omelette for our mother (who doesn't eat fish or meat) and reluctantly chose the buffet for the rest of us.

The buffet was extremely disappointing — bland and tasteless. To make matters worse, I found a piece of hair in one of the dishes. After we had already paid, I discreetly showed it to the server. Shockingly, there was no apology or acknowledgment of the issue.

We didn't make a formal complaint, but it was clear to us that this establishment is poorly managed and runs more like a money-making operation than a hospitality service. Just three days earlier, we had a much better experience at the small Avanhala restaurant opposite this place — friendlier service and better food.

Ambepussa Rest House needs serious improvements in cleanliness, customer service, and food quality to live up to even basic expectations.

description: Sacred, historic temple said to be visited by the Buddha with paintings by a renowned artist.
display_review:
A Sacred Treasure of Sri Lankan Buddhism
The Kelaniya Raja Maha Vihara is one of Sri Lanka's most ancient and revered Buddhist temples, constantly filled with devoted worshippers and curious visitors from around the world. This sacred site is not just a place of worship—it's a living masterpiece of art and history.
What makes this temple truly extraordinary are the stunning murals and hand-painted artworks that adorn its walls. These paintings showcase the unique style of Sri Lankan Buddhist art, created by master artists whose work has drawn admirers from across the globe. The intricate detail, vibrant colors, and spiritual storytelling in these murals are unlike anything you'll find elsewhere. Art enthusiasts and historians travel from far and wide specifically to witness these remarkable creations.
The temple grounds are always bustling with activity, but there's something magical about visiting in the evening. As the sun sets, the temple comes alive with a serene beauty—the soft lighting, the sound of evening prayers, and the peaceful atmosphere create an unforgettable experience.
What makes Kelaniya Raja Maha Vihara especially sacred is that it houses precious relics of Lord Buddha, enshrined within the temple's pagoda (dagoba). According to tradition, the Buddha himself visited this site during his third visit to Sri Lanka, making it one of the most significant pilgrimage sites for Buddhists.

Practical Tips for Visitors:
Don't forget to buy flowers to offer to the Buddha—there are plenty of flower vendors available near the parking area. The temple has ample parking space, but it's important to note: do not leave your valuables in the vehicle. It's highly recommended to carry your belongings with you or have someone stay with the vehicle for security.
Whether you're a devotee seeking spiritual solace or a traveler interested in culture and history, this temple offers a profound and beautiful experience. Highly recommended for anyone visiting Sri Lanka!
review_1: Many locals are seen praying here. Wall paintings and idols have cultural significance. It is very famous as it is a place where King Walagamba lived. A very ancient historical value is found in the Lenagala Raja Maha Viharaya. Moreover, this temple can be taken as an example to honor the Sinhalese nation.
review_2: As the presence of an early-Brahmi cave inscription, the history of Lenagala can be dated back to the pre-Christian era. Locals also link the history of this temple to King Valagamba [(103 B.C. and c. 89–77 B.C.) Abeyawardana, 2002]. It is evident from the murals and sculptures in the cave temple that this Viharaya was renovated during the Kandyan Period (1597-1815 A.D.).
review_3: Amazing historical place to visit. Has ancient arts and buddha statues done by ancient time period. Calm environment and easy to visit by vehicle.
review_4:
Lenagala Rajamaha Viharaya at Dedigama is a unique cave temple built on a picturesque hill under a massive cave. It is also home to the largest cave inscription in the Western Province.

The large brahmi inscription of the Lenagala Rajamaha Viharaya lies on the ledge of the large cave.  47 large characters are caved on the first line and some more characters can be seen few feet below, carved in 2 lines. The letters are about 7 inches tall and carved 1 inch deep in to the rock.

The history of this cave temple goes as far as early Anuradhapura period. It is the general belief that this temple was first built by king Wattagamini Abhaya, better known as king Walagamba (89-77 BC). The temple has been last renovated by the popular Satara Korale Lewke Dissawe during the Kandyan era.

However the inscription at the dripledge of the cave has been determined to be of 2nd century BC dating this temple even further back in history. This inscription mentions that the cave Manapadassana of prince Duhatara, son of prince Siva, Son of prince Duhatara is offered to the sangha of four quarters present and absent (Granted to the cave are) – One lot in Anudigama one lot in Batasha Nagara. This inscription is written in Old Sinhala language using early Brahmi Script.

Lewke Wijewardhana (Wijesundara) Rajakaruna became the Dissawe of the Satara Korale in 1802 during the period of king Sri Wickrama Rajasinhe of Kandyan kingdom. He was renowned for his extraordinary strength, skills in arm to arm combat and for swordsmanship. Many stories are still being told of his various extraordinary abilities including his ability to squeeze open a coconut and swinging a sward dipped in lime chalk so that the tip touched every spectator leaving a trace of lime on their body. In addition to his skills, he was  a devoted Buddhist who had renovated and supported many temples in the area including the Lenagala Rajamaha Viharaya.

There is a fairly detailed description of the HCP Bell’s 1892 report on the Kegalle District commonly known as the Kegalle Report.

The cave is divided in to 3 viharayas. The left most temple is called “Sath Sathi Viharaya” . This is 15 feet 10 inches long and 12 feet wide. Entrance of the viharaya is made of large granite pillars carved with foliage and other designs. This entrance is about 6 feet 8 inches tall and 4 feet 2 inches wide and 7 inches thick. All the Buddha statues show features of Kandyan era. Since most of them have not be re painted, the artistic traditions of the Kandyan era is still well preserved. A granite door frame can be seen in front of one of the standing statues in this viharaya. This is believed to be the entrance to a small room which housed the image in earlier times.

The center viharaya called the “Maha Lena” is the largest of the three. This is 31 feet 6 inches long 17 feet 9 inches wide. Entrance of the viharaya is made of large granite pillars carved with foliage. The top column is caved with a bosath image and with deities of Saman, Vishnu and Davatha Bandara. The bottom of the pillars are caved with lions with open jaws. This entrance is 11 feet 6 inches high, 8 feet wide and 9 inches thick.  All the Buddha statues show features of Kandyan era. The main statue in this viharaya is a large reclining Buddha statue. This is 29 feet long and 6 feet wide at the shoulders. On the left wall you will find standing figures of Buddha and deity Vishnu. On the right wall lies another standing Buddha statue with statues of deities Vishnu and Saman. The cave roof is painted with lotus flower designs.
review_5: This is an ancient temple that made in a cave. There are many ancient paintings of load Buddha's life in that caves

description: The Buddha is said to have attained enlightenment under this fig tree that is now a pilgrimage site.
display_review:
Humbling and Inspiring Experience

Our group of thirteen, travelling with Flamingo Lanka, arrived at the sacred site of Sri Maha Bodhi in the afternoon on 21st November 2025.  The 500 metre walk from the designated car park was through well-kept pathways shaded by many other large trees.  Halfway to the bo tree, we reached a shoe counter where we left our shoes for safekeeping at no charge.  Continuing barefoot, we joined a stream of pilgrims, mostly dressed in white, carrying offerings of flowers and incense.

The Sri Maha Bodhi is recognised as the oldest living human-planted tree in the world, having been planted in 288BC.  It originated from a cutting of the Bo tree in Bodh Gaya, India - the very tree under which Lord Buddha attained enlightenment.  The sapling was brought to Sri Lanka by Sanghamitta, a buddhist nun, and ceremoniously received by the king marking a significant moment in the island’s religious history.

Over the centuries, the Sri Maha Bodhi has been continuously protected and cared for by Buddhist monks, devoted kings and now by the government.  It stands surrounded by golden railings on a high platform, serving as the spiritual heart of Anuradhapura.  For more than 2300 years, this sacred tree has been venerated by Buddhists from around the world, making it one of the most revered pilgrimage sites in Sri Lanka.

Long before skyscrapers and highways, there was Anuradhapura — a sacred city where kings, monks, and pilgrims walked under the same Bodhi tree that still grows today.

More than two thousand years ago, a small sapling from the original Bodhi Tree in India was brought here. It was planted, cared for, and protected by generations — and it still stands, alive and thriving, making Anuradhapura one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world.

As the morning sun rises over the white stupas, their domes glow like pearls against the deep blue sky. The air is quiet, filled with the distant sound of temple bells and the whisper of devotees offering flowers.

display_review:
Buddhist Pilgrimage Site

On November 22, 2025, thirteen of us travelling with Flamingo Lanka visited the revered Abhayagiri stupa.  It is one of the most significant and monumental landmarks in the ancient capitol Anuradhapura.

Abhyagiri was once a thriving center of Buddhist learning and monastic life.  Established in the 1st century BC by King Walagamba, it grew into a renowned international institution, attracting scholars from distant lands. The Chinese monk, Faxian, who visited in the 5th century recorded that the complex housed 5000 monks.

The main attraction today is the Abhayagiri Stupa, standing 75 metres tall with a circumference of 350 metres.  Over the centuries, the stupa had been renovated and restored maintaining its grandeur and spiritual significance

Abhayagiri remains a deeply sacred site for Buddhists and a powerful symbol of faith.  Following tradition, we walked clockwise around the stupa.  Buddhists among us, joined the crowds offering flowers and chanting pirith - Buddhist stanzas - venerating Buddha and his teachings creating a serene, peaceful and a tranquil atmosphere.

review_2: Abhayagiri, important ancient Theravāda Buddhist monastic centre (vihāra) built by King Vaṭṭagāmaṇi Abhaya (29–17 BC) on the northern side of Anurādhapura, the capital of Ceylon (Sri Lanka) at that time. Its importance lay, in part, in the fact that religious and political power were closely related, so that monastic centres had much influence on the secular history of the nation. But it is also important in the history of Theravāda Buddhism itself. It was originally associated with the nearby Mahāvihāra (“Great Monastery”), which was the traditional centre of religious and civil power built by Devānanpiya-Tissa (307–267 BC). But Abhayagiri seceded from the Great Monastery toward the end of Vaṭṭagāmaṇi’s reign in a dispute over the relations between monks and the lay community
